title: feature request: looping over blocks
body:
##### Issue Type:

Feature Idea

##### Component Name:
blocks

##### Ansible Version:

Ansible 2.0.0_rc-1
##### Ansible Configuration:

NA
##### Environment:

Ubuntu 15.10

##### Summary of Decision:

We're open to implementing this but want it to go through the proposal process.  Please see: https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/13262#issuecomment-335904803 for details.

##### Summary:

There are a number of use-cases where it would be valuable to be able to loop over a block of tasks, such that a few tasks are done in order, and that specific block of tasks are looped over for some set of values. It seems that the new block functionality could lend itself well to this if you were to enable looping over blocks.
##### Steps To Reproduce:

``` yaml
- hosts: localhost
  connection: local
  tasks:
  - block:
    - debug: msg="task 1 loop {{item}}"
    - debug: msg="task 2 loop {{item}}"
    with_items:
    - "1"
    - "2"
```
##### Expected Results:

```
PLAY ***************************************************************************

TASK [setup] *******************************************************************
ok: [localhost]

TASK [debug msg=task 1 loop {{item}}] ******************************************
ok: [localhost] => {
    "changed": false, 
    "msg": "task 1 loop 1"
}

TASK [debug msg=task 2 loop {{item}}] ******************************************
ok: [localhost] => {
    "changed": false, 
    "msg": "task 2 loop 1"
}

TASK [debug msg=task 1 loop {{item}}] ******************************************
ok: [localhost] => {
    "changed": false, 
    "msg": "task 1 loop 2"
}

TASK [debug msg=task 2 loop {{item}}] ******************************************
ok: [localhost] => {
    "changed": false, 
    "msg": "task 2 loop 2"
}

PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************
localhost                  : ok=5    changed=0    unreachable=0    failed=0
```
##### Actual Results:

```
ERROR! 'with_items' is not a valid attribute for a Block

The error appears to have been in '/root/test.yml': line 5, column 5, but may
be elsewhere in the file depending on the exact syntax problem.

The offending line appears to be:

  tasks:
  - block:
    ^ here
```

title: Allow ssh private keys to be used that are encrypted with vault
body:
<!---
Verify first that your issue/request is not already reported on GitHub.
Also test if the latest release, and master branch are affected too.
-->

##### ISSUE TYPE
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Name of the module/plugin/task/feature -->
Core library specific to ssh authentication

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from “ansible --version” between quotes below -->
```
ansible 2.2.0.0
  config file = .../ansible/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = ['./library']
```

##### CONFIGURATION
<!---
Mention any settings you have changed/added/removed in ansible.cfg
(or using the ANSIBLE_* environment variables).
-->
I am proposing a new variable(ansible_ssh_private_key) be added as an alternative to (ansible_ssh_private_key_file).

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
<!---
Mention the OS you are running Ansible from, and the OS you are
managing, or say “N/A” for anything that is not platform-specific.
-->
N/A
##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->
When defining hosts, I would like to be able to use an ssh private key that is stored securely using ansible-vault.  There are basically 3 ways to achieve this and I have listed them below:

1. Add `ansible_ssh_private_key` variable that can be a string version of the private key (this uses the already allowed vaulting of groupt_vars / host_vars files)

2. Add `ansible_ssh_private_key_vault_file` that is a file that is encrypted with ansible-vault and decrypted at run time to provide the key to the ssh agent.

3. Change how `ansible_ssh_private_key_file` works so that it first attempts to decrypt using ansible-vault when it is encrypted.  

Personally, I don't care which way this feature is added and I am more than willing to write the code for it.  I just want to know what and if the community / devs want this feature added.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, show exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case.
For new features, show how the feature would be used.
-->

I am going to reference each of the solutions individually on how they would be used.

1. (ansible_ssh_private_key) 
inventory/development.ini
```ini
[atlanta]
host1
host2
```

inventory/group_vars/atlanta.yml (showing as decrypted obviously)
```yaml
ansible_ssh_private_key: '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----so secret much secure-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----'
```

2. (ansible_ssh_private_key_vault_file) 
inventory/development.ini
```ini
[atlanta]
host1 ansible_ssh_private_key_vault_file="{{ inventory_dir }}/atlanta.key"
host2 ansible_ssh_private_key_file="{{ inventory_dir }}/atlanta.key"
```

inventory/atlanta.key (showing as decrypted obviously)
```
-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
so secret much secure
-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----'
```

3. (extending ansible_ssh_private_key_file to use vault first) 
inventory/development.ini
```ini
[atlanta]
host1 an

title: Add support for using blocks as handlers
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Core

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
N/A

##### SUMMARY
Currently it's not possible to define multiple tasks for one handler. It is possible to call multiple handlers, or to notify the next handler from the first one, but all of these are more workarounds, than simple solutions. Since Ansbile 2.0 added support for blocks, maybe we could add this support to handlers, so we could write something like this:

```
- name: reload nginx
  block:
    - command: nginx -t
    - service: name=nginx state=reloaded
```

I'm not sure if this would be the correct syntax, but would be really nice to have.

title: Conditionally import_playbook aka include_playbook
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
import_playbook

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from "ansible --version" between quotes below -->
```
2.4.0.0
```

##### CONFIGURATION
<!---
If using Ansible 2.4 or above, paste the results of "ansible-config dump --only-changed"
Otherwise, mention any settings you have changed/added/removed in ansible.cfg
(or using the ANSIBLE_* environment variables).
-->

n/a

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

CentOS 6

##### SUMMARY

I need to conditionally import a playbook, which isn't possible.  The keyword I'm missing is "include_playbook" which would allow a "when" to apply to it.  Why?

I have a playbook that performs some maintenance, OS, Kernel, package, and firmware upgrades.  For Major upgrades, we track progress in a ticketing system.  So, during those runs, I'd like to conditionally import a playbook that updates the ticket information.  In order for that to work, I need to pass the user's password to the ticketing system, so there's a `vars_prompt` in the ticket update playbook.  If the user specifies which ticket they're working to the maintenance playbook, I'd like the ticket update playbook to be called after the maintenance is performed.

If there's another way for this to work, I'm open to alternate ideas, but I think conditionally playbook imports would be generically useful.  I'm not understanding why tasks could be included dynamically, but a playbook wouldn't be.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, show exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case.
For new features, show how the feature would be used.
-->

<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
# Maintenance Play Runs first, then conditionally import a second playbook
- import_playbook: update-ticket.yaml
  when: ticket_id is defined
```

<!--- You can also paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
<!--- What did you expect to happen when running the steps above? -->

I expect `update-ticket.yaml` to only import if the `ticket_id` is defined.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
<!--- What actually happened? If possible run with extra verbosity (-vvvv) -->

no parse error, and playbook `update-ticket.yaml` is imported 100% of the time.

title: Allow decrypting of files with vaulted variables
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ansible-vault

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.3.1.0
  config file = 
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
  python version = 2.7.13 (default, Apr 23 2017, 16:50:35) [GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 8.0.0 (clang-800.0.42.1)]
```

##### CONFIGURATION
n/a

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
n/a

##### SUMMARY
ansible-vault decrypt allows the decryption of completely encrypted yaml files, but it will not decrypt vaulted variables in an unencrypted yaml file with encrypted variables.

It would be nice, for CLI purposes, to have decrypt take a partially encrypted file, and give us the decrypted text.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
* create `test.yml` file with single encrypted variable encrypted by `~/.vault_pass.txt`
* ansible-vault decrypt file

```
ansible-vault decrypt test.yml --vault-password-file ~/.vault_pass.txt
```

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
* Expected plain text output with encrypted variable decrypted.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
```
ERROR! input is not vault encrypted data for test.yml
```

title: Add state=empty into file module
body:
_From @Nindaleth on March 6, 2015 17:2_

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
file


##### SUMMARY
In our playbooks we need to empty some folders, but currently our only option seems to be using `command` or `shell` module. It has its uses to leave the folder intact as there may have been some owner/permission changes, or alternatively it may be a mountpoint that is not even possible to delete.
Similar ideas might be had about files as well.

##### PROPOSED BEHAVIOUR
- Directories: remove all files and subdirectories inside so that the directory contains nothing
- Files: truncate the target file, if it was empty then touch it
- Symlinks/Hardlinks: check which of the previous 2 types it is, act accordingly

State change changed if previously non-empty, ok if previously empty, failed if still non-empty.

##### EXAMPLE USAGE
```yaml
- name: Clean folders
  file: path=/var/zookeeper state=empty

- name: Truncate logs
  file: path=/var/log/local6 state=empty
```

##### WORKAROUND
How to empty a directory completely:
```yaml
- find: path=/my/dir file_type=file
  register: files

- find: path=/my/dir file_type=directory
  register: directories

- file: state=absent path={{ item.path }}
  with_items: 
    - "{{ files }}"
    - "{{ directories }}"
```

Not sure if there is a reasonable workaround for emptying _multiple_ directories.

_Copied from original issue: ansible/ansible-modules-core#902_

title: Add 'update' option to ansible-galaxy
body:
##### Issue Type:

Feature Idea
##### Ansible Version:

ansible 1.5
##### Environment:

N/A
##### Summary:

As [mentioned](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/ansible-devel/o_-WIDQWKqo) on the mailing list, the `ansible-galaxy` command has `install` and `remove` options, but no `update` option.

The solution is currently to use `ansible-galaxy install [role],[version] --force`, but that feels a little strange, and isn't necessarily obvious.

Could we add an update command that works like `ansible-galaxy update [role],[version]` (where `,[version]` is optional, and specifies a particular version to download), and simply updates to the latest version of the module available—or, if it's already the latest, no update is performed?
##### Steps To Reproduce:

N/A

N/A

N/A

title: "retries" without "until": retry until success
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

retries

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
ansible 2.2.1.0
  config file =
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```

##### CONFIGURATION

N/A

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

N/A

##### SUMMARY

At the moment, giving "retries:" without an "until:" condition has no effect.

It would be useful if this could be implemented to mean "retry until success"

(Or if not, at least generate an error saying that `retries` has no effect without `until`)

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

```yaml
- hosts: localhost
  tasks:
  - file: path=/tmp/zzz state=absent

  - name: testing timeout
    get_url:
      url: http://1.2.3.4/zzz
      dest: /tmp/zzz
    retries: 2
    delay: 3
```

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

The module is retried 2 times.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS

The module is run only once.

```
PLAY [localhost] ***************************************************************

TASK [setup] *******************************************************************
ok: [localhost]

TASK [file] ********************************************************************
ok: [localhost]

TASK [testing timeout] *********************************************************
fatal: [localhost]: FAILED! => {"changed": false, "dest": "/tmp/zzz", "failed": true, "msg": "Request failed: <urlopen error timed out>", "state": "absent", "url": "http://1.2.3.4/zzz"}
	to retry, use: --limit @/home/ubuntu/ert.retry
```

To get the desired behaviour you have to add three lines:

```
    register: result
    until: result|succeeded
    ignore_errors: yes
```

But an unfortunate side-effect of `ignore_errors` is that a failure even after all the retries is ignored:

```
TASK [testing timeout] *********************************************************
FAILED - RETRYING: TASK: testing timeout (2 retries left).
FAILED - RETRYING: TASK: testing timeout (1 retries left).
fatal: [localhost]: FAILED! => {"attempts": 2, "changed": false, "dest": "/tmp/zzz", "failed": true, "msg": "Request failed: <urlopen error timed out>", "state": "absent", "url": "http://1.2.3.4/zzz"}
...ignoring
```

So you need to add another task, something like this:

```
  - name: report error
    fail:
      msg: 'Unable to download {{result.url}}: {{result.response|default(result.msg)}}'
    when: not result|succeeded
```

This is pretty messy when all you wanted to do is say "if this task fails, retry it up to N times and then give up"

title: Cowsay should not be enabled by default
body:
Having cows fill my screen when running a playbook is not the expected behavior, and makes it hard to visually parse the output of ansible. Since cowsay is enabled not by a setting, but just by the presence of the cowsay binary on the system, it is a surprise whether or not cows will appear when I run ansible-playbook on a new system.

Instead of putting the burden of setting an environment variable on people who don't want cows, the default should be normal text output with no cows. The environment variable should be renamed from "ANSIBLE_NOCOWS" to "ANSIBLE_COWS". This way, we get a sane, unsurprising default, but people can still get their cows if they so desire.

https://github.com/ansible/ansible/pull/10531

title: support for "serial" on an individual task
body:
(Feature Idea)

The natural way to handle configuration or other updates that require a rolling restart would be to perform updates in parallel, then notify a handler, which performs a restart with `serial`. But this is not possible, requiring either manual rolling restart or ugly hacks. See https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/ansible-project/rBcWzXjt-Xc

title: Feature: Allow until-loops on blocks or includes
body:
##### SUMMARY
It would be quite useful if you can loop over more than one single tasks.

For instance if you have to poll a remote system for some progress and at the same time you want to push this progress to another backend, you could be doing:

```yaml
- hosts: localhost
  tasks:
  - name: Start a long-running task
    uri:
      url: https://some-service/v1/put/new_job
      body: { foo: bar }
    register: new_job

  - until: job_status.json.message in ['Finished', 'Failed']
    block:
    - name: Get job status
      uri:
        url: https://some-service/v1/get/new_job
      register: job_status

    - name: Report job status to web service
      uri:
        url: https://backend-system/v1/post/job_status
        body: '{{ job_status.json }}'
```

There are many uses to this.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Core

title: Allow use of async on blocks
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
`block` and `async`

##### SUMMARY
It would be awesome if it was possible to use `async` with `block`s. I've got four things to set up, and they can be set up in parallel, but they have internal steps that have to be done in order.

Example use case:

```yaml
- name: build thing1
  async: 300
  poll: 0
  register: build1
  block:
    - name: clone # big repo, takes 30 secs
      git: repo=ssh://git@github.com/org/repo1.git dest=/source/repo1 version="master" accept_hostkey=yes
    - name: npm install # lots to install, takes a minute
      command: chdir=/source/repo1 npm install 
    - name: npm run build # takes 3 minutes
      shell: chdir=/source/repo2 npm run build
- name: build thing2
  async: 300
  poll: 0
  register: build2
  block:
    - name: clone
      git: repo=ssh://git@github.com/org/repo2.git dest=/source/repo2 version="master" accept_hostkey=yes
    - name: npm install
      command: chdir=/source/repo2 npm install 
    - name: npm run build # takes 3 minutes
      shell: chdir=/source/repo2 npm run build

# do other stuff, then at the end:

- name: Wait for build1 to complete
  async_status: jid={{build1.ansible_job_id}}
  register: result
  until: result.finished
  retries: 300
- name: Wait for build2 to complete
  async_status: jid={{build2.ansible_job_id}}
  register: result
  until: result.finished
  retries: 300
```

Obviously I need to clone the repo before I can run `npm i`, and I need to `npm i` before I can `npm build`. But there's no reason these two repos can't be cloned, installed and built in parallel for speed gains. And more speed gains if there are even more repos :)

title: Ansible Vault should be able to specify the vault password in an environment variable
body:
##### SUMMARY

Allow an environment variable to be set, e.g. ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD which if set will be tried to unlock any vault-encrypted secrets

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Ansible Vault / vault password environment variable

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
Currently vault encrypted secrets require that:

*  `--ask-vault-password` be specified which makes it interactive and non-automatable without using something like expect OR
* `--vault-password-file` be used which violates compliance (Most security compliance require that passwords are not written down in files in plaintext anywhere, even temporarily).

Of the three other (simple) ways of getting information into a system, STDIN (through a pipe), commandline args, and environment variables:

* STDIN abstracts the problem to another tool, but is a horrible solution an you only get one STDIN to play with.
* Commandline args are visible in the process tree so is no good for security purposes
* Environment variables are secure (can only be read by user and root, like the memory itself), and provide an ideal mechanism for setting a password for automatation purposes. 

It would be great if ansible vault could therefore support an env var such as ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD which a user could set through whatever means they choose, to supply ansible with the vault password to decrypt the secrets at runtime.

I'm not sure why the file methods was chosen as this undoes the work of ansible-vault in encrypting data at rest, by providing the password in data at rest. If therefore my disk is accessed or my machine is stolen, someone gains the passwords anyway. 

See for example "Passwords or pass phrases must not be written down or stored in readable format." in the wikipedia version of https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO/IEC_27002

  154         if docker_version < MIN_DOCKER_VERSION:

This is how python compares strings:

False

title: Support running ansible-test on collections outside a collection root
body:
##### SUMMARY

When I develop my Ansible Collections, I want to be able to use a typical development workflow:

  1. `git clone [my project] [local folder]`
  2. `cd [local folder]`
  3. `ansible-test integration` (or whatever other command for local testing)

Currently, if I do this I get the following error:

```
 10:23:35 ~/Dropbox/VMs/collections/geerlingguy.php_roles $ ansible-test
ERROR: The current working directory must be at or below one of:

 - Ansible source: /Users/jgeerling/Downloads/ansible/
 - Ansible collection: {...}/ansible_collections/{namespace}/{collection}/

Current working directory: /Users/jgeerling/Dropbox/VMs/collections/geerlingguy.php_roles
```

I feel like this presents a pretty significant burden on collection maintainers and contributors, because a _very_ common workflow (especially if I'm not on my main workstation for some reason) is: clone repo, hack on it, run integration tests, commit changes, push to PR.

If I have to create an arbitrary directory structure, then clone into that structure, it's a lot of overhead. Other existing testing tools like `molecule` (or in other languages, Mocha for Node, PHPUnit or Behat for PHP, etc.) are self-contained and run from within the project directory without issue...

title: ansible json module for editing json files
body:
##### SUMMARY
Ansible really needs a JSON module that can edit json file and perform atomic operations like
- add entry (and create path if needed)
- delete entry
- replace entry

##### COMPONENT NAME
json

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
As Ansible is primary a configuration management tool, it needs to be able to alter configuration files in order to enable or disable different features.

`ini` and `line_in_file` had proved very useful over time but they cannot realiably be used to update json files. 

Lots of tools do keep their configuration in JSON format and one practical example is docker config which on synology looks like:
```
$ cat /var/packages/Docker/etc/dockerd.json
{
   "hosts" : ["unix:///var/run/docker.sock" ],
  "registry-mirrors" : []
}
```

If you want to make it available using tcp  you hour have to add `"tcp://127.0.0.1:2375"` to the hosts list.

At this moment this cannot be done.

I know that over time templating was proposed as a way to avoid having to update config files: this does not work because it assumes you know what the file contains which is an incorrect assumption. The tool config can be changed by the user, another, a version update, or maybe even another ansible role. If you use templates you guarantee future failure.

While implementing this we could look at two of popular tools used to update jsonfiles: [json from npm and jq](https://stackoverflow.com/a/43292244/99834), the former one aso being name as "sed for json".

title: 'immediate' handlers (feature idea)
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
core

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
N/A

##### SUMMARY

Ansible's roles and handlers start hitting limitations when one role wants to fire actions implemented by another role immediately, not just at the end of a play.

handlers that can be declared immediate, so that they fire as soon as notified, would be very useful when composing multiple roles. For example, one role might need to perform tasks that requires restarting of services defined in another role multiple times throughout the series of tasks.

As far as I can tell, at the moment this must be done by exposing task snippets in one role to be used by inclusion in the second role, using `includes;`.

It would potentially be a lot cleaner to be able to invoke handlers, e.g. to rerun configuration templates, restart services multiple times throughout a play, etc.

title: dnf: support config-manager plugin for repository management
body:
<!--- Verify first that your feature was not already discussed on GitHub -->
<!--- Complete *all* sections as described, this form is processed automatically -->

##### SUMMARY
The dnf config-manager plugin is the means of managing the actual rpm repositories available to dnf. In Fedora it's use for managing installed but not enabled by default repositories such as updates-testing and some media codecs. In RHEL these repositories are used extensively for extras/optional and other such repos.

dnf config-manager supports the following options that would be useful to manage using ansible:

* name - name to give to the rep, or the name of an existing repo
* add-repo - URL of the repo
* set-enabled - enable the repo specified by the name
* set-disabled - disable the repo specified by the name
* set-opt proxy - configure a proxy for the specified repo(s)

https://dnf-plugins-core.readthedocs.io/en/latest/config_manager.html

##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
dnf module

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
dnf provides a lot of repos and while the ansible dnf module enables you to manage installing/upgrading/removing packages and you can temporarily enable a repo as part of that process there is no means to manage the repositories themselves, so for example if you enable a repository to install a particular package but forget to enable that repository when applying updates you may miss critical updates to the packages that are installed by ansible. By managing being able to the repository you can enable the repository at the beginning of a task (eg fedora-cisco-openh264), install the package using the default means (ie not enabling the repo in the install component), and updates automatically get applied as part of an upgrade task without having to track what special usecases enabled repos as part of an install task.

title: Add an option lock_wait to the apt module
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea 
This is a copy of the issue on the old repository: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/issues/4717
There was some work on it, I don't know why it's not added: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/pull/4842

##### COMPONENT NAME
apt

##### SUMMARY
This is a feature request but also works around a problem that is very common when manipulating apt packages/repositories on a given host from multiple instances of apt.

Currently if there is an instance working with apt (via ansible apt module or not) there is a lock which is being held exclusively and as such no other apt instance can get a hold of. The result is that if there's a second instance of apt which tries to acquire the lock it gets an instant failure. In the case of doing this via ansible the result is:

msg: Failed to lock apt for exclusive operation

This is the default apt behavior and as far as I can tell there is not way to pass a flag to apt command line, or apt configuraion, to tell it to wait for the lock to be released and then only timeout after a certain period of time.

It would be very useful if ansible's apt module itself was to provide such a feature. Say an optional flag - for example lock_wait=60 which would make ansible try to acquire the lock every second for X seconds and eventually timing out. This way if two ansible playbooks running at the same time are trying to use the apt repository they will have much higher chance of succeeding.

Currently one of them errors out only so that the user retry the entire playbook .

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
Run two ansible instances each utilizing the apt module at the very same time - one of them will fail with the above error.

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
If we have this feature in ansible then we don't need to wrap each individual playbook's apt calls with retries and have it built-in the apt module itself.

title: Make 'ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml' idempotent
body:
##### Issue Type:

Bug Report/Feature Idea
##### Ansible Version:

```
ansible 1.9.1
  configured module search path = None
```
##### Ansible Configuration:

``` ini
[defaults]                                                                      
log_path = ../.tmp/ansible.log                                                  
# path to the directory containing roles                                        
roles_path = roles                                                              
hash_behaviour = merge                                                          
error_on_undefined_vars = yes                                                   
host_key_checking=False
```
##### Environment:

N/A
##### Summary:

Running `ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml` should be idempotent (e.g. it should behave like `pip install -r requirements.txt`).
##### Steps To Reproduce:
1. Create `requirements.yml` file with the following contents:
   
   ``` yaml
   # nodejs role
   - src: https://github.com/nodesource/ansible-nodejs-role
     version: 592a239dca52e1c5ff79cfefd0072efe3831428e
     name: nodejs
   ```
2. Run `ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml`.
3. Re-run `ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml`.
##### Expected Results:

``` bash
[tadej@tlinux64 admin]$ ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml
- executing: git clone https://github.com/nodesource/ansible-nodejs-role nodejs
- executing: git archive --prefix=nodejs/ --output=/tmp/tmpVoJEMx.tar 592a239dca52e1c5ff79cfefd0072efe3831428e
- extracting nodejs to roles/nodejs
- nodejs was installed successfully
```

On the second run, `ansible-galaxy` should detect that the `nodejs` role is already installed and make sure the installed version matches the version specified in `requirements.yml`.
##### Actual Results:

``` bash
[tadej@tlinux64 admin]$ ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml
- executing: git clone https://github.com/nodesource/ansible-nodejs-role nodejs
- executing: git archive --prefix=nodejs/ --output=/tmp/tmpVoJEMx.tar 592a239dca52e1c5ff79cfefd0072efe3831428e
- extracting nodejs to roles/nodejs
- nodejs was installed successfully
[tadej@tlinux64 admin]$ ansible-galaxy install -r requirements.yml
- executing: git clone https://github.com/nodesource/ansible-nodejs-role nodejs
- executing: git archive --prefix=nodejs/ --output=/tmp/tmp73DSKM.tar 592a239dca52e1c5ff79cfefd0072efe3831428e
- extracting nodejs to roles/nodejs
- error: the specified role nodejs appears to already exist. Use --force to replace it.
- nodejs was NOT installed successfully.
- you can use --ignore-errors to skip failed roles.
```
##### Additional Info:
- One can specify `--ignore-errors` to ignore the failed roles, however, this is not the appropriate solution, since this would also ignore errors where `ansible-galaxy` failed to fetch a role from a remote server.
- It should be possible to obtain the version of the installed role via `meta/.galaxy_install_info` file. For example, for the `nodejs` role

title: Unsetting variables
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ansible

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.3.0.0
  config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
  python version = 2.7.12 (default, Nov 19 2016, 06:48:10) [GCC 5.4.0 20160609]
```

##### CONFIGURATION
Defaults.

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY
I need a way to unset a variable, but there is none. My usecase is:
1. Run a playbook with some settings.
2. ... some jobs happen ...
3. Preserve some variables (`set_fact: { preserve: { ansible_port: '{{ ansible_port || default(ommit) }}' } }`).
4. Set `ansible_port` to other value.
5. Do what's required.
6. Set `ansible_port` back to preserved value.
7. Do the rest with `ansible_port` the same as during (2).

The problem is that in (6) I can't unset the variable if it was not defined (and thus was ommited) at (3).
Setting `ansible_port` to `None` does not work, and the behaviour I want is to use system ssh client port setting there (and to not just hardcode it to 22). Furthermore, there are also other variables that has to be treated that way.

I propose adding a special variable value called `unset` (or `undefine`) to allow for fact undefining.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

```yaml
- name: Define a fact
  set_fact:
    ansible_port: 22

- name: Undefine a fact
  set_fact:
    ansible_port: '{{ unset }}'

- debug:
    var: ansible_port
```

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
A variable to be undefined.

```
TASK [debug] ***************************************************************************************************************************************************************
ok: [host] => {
    "ansible_port": "VARIABLE IS NOT DEFINED!",
    "changed": false
}
```

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
It didn't work.

```
TASK [Undefine a fact] *****************************************************************************************************************************************************
fatal: [host]: FAILED! => {"failed": true, "msg": "the field 'args' has an invalid value, which appears to include a variable that is undefined. The error was: 'unset' is undefined\n\nThe error appears to have been in '/ansible/test.yml': line x, column y, but may\nbe elsewhere in the file depending on the exact syntax problem.\n\nThe offending line appears to be:\n\n  tasks:\n    - name: Undefine a fact\n      ^ here\n"}
        to retry, use: --limit @/ansible/test.retry
```

title: Support specifying collections in git repositories in requirements.yml
body:
##### SUMMARY
When I **develop collections**, I like to store them in git repositories. Once they reach a stable point, I usually tag them and push them to Galaxy.

It would be a lot more convenient if `ansible-galaxy` CLI command would support specifying a **collection requirement in `requirements.yml`** that would **reference** any git "treeish" object (i.e. tag, branch, commit, ...) in a **git repository**.

---

Another use case is when a company creates some **private collections** for in-house use and **doesn't want** to deploy a **full Ansible Galaxy server** to be able to elegantly use them from a `requirements.yml` file.

Again, it would be a lot more convenient if `ansible-galaxy` CLI command would support specifying a **collection requirement in `requirements.yml`** that would **reference** any git "treeish" object (i.e. tag, branch, commit, ...) in a **private git repository**.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ansible-galaxy CLI

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
The exact same thing is actually already supported when [specifying a role to install in the `requirements.yml` file](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/reference_appendices/galaxy.html#installing-multiple-roles-from-a-file).

I would suggest the same or similar syntax, e.g.:

```yaml
collections:
  - name: my_namespace.my_collection
    src: git@git.company.com:my_namespace/ansible-my-collection.git
    scm: git
    version: "1.2.3"
```

Like with roles, some keys could be omitted and the CLI would use some sensible defaults.

There might be some ambiguity between the `src` key and the [existing `source` key which is used to specify the Galaxy URL from which to pull the collection](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/devel/dev_guide/collections_tech_preview.html#install-multiple-collections-with-a-requirements-file). But it should be solvable and the details should be worked-out when preparing the implementation.

From [what I read](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/devel/dev_guide/collections_tech_preview.html#collection-versions), collections have a stricter requirement on the version than the old roles and require using Semantic Versioning. This again should be solvable since an appropriate version must be specified in the `galaxy.yml` in collection's root.

title: feature-request/Add 'remote_src' option to 'template' module
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Files/Template

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
ansible 2.3.2.0 (stable-2.3 10aa164166) last updated 2017/06/29 18:32:01 (GMT +300)
  config file = /home/user/.ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
  python version = 2.7.5 (default, Nov  6 2016, 00:28:07) [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-11)]
```

##### CONFIGURATION
none
##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY
Lots of application, I install with ansible, are tar archives downloaded with 'maven_artifact' or 'unarchive' modules.
Some of these archives contains templates of configuration files in jinja2 format. I want to be able copy those files to application config directory with replacing jinja2 placeholders and without copying them to my local machine.
Now I have to use module 'replace', but I think it may be more native to use 'template' module with option 'remote_src=yes'

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, show exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case.
For new features, show how the feature would be used.
-->

<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
- name: Get Snapshot app
  maven_artifact:
    group_id: "{{ component_group_id }}"
    artifact_id: "{{ component_artifact_id }}"
    repository_url: "{{ snapshot_repository }}"
    version: "{{ component_version }}"
    extension: 'tar.gz'
    dest: "{{ some_path }}.tgz"

- name: Extract tarball
  unarchive:
    remote_src: yes
    src: "{{ some_path }}.tgz"
    dest: "{{ app_directory }}"

- name: Copy configuration files
  template:
    remote_src: yes  # <- this is what I want
    src: "{{ app_directory }}/conf_templates/{{ item }}.j2"
    dest: "{{ app_directory }}/conf/{{ item }}"
  with_items: "{{ conf_files }}"
```

<!--- You can also paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
<!--- What did you expect to happen when running the steps above? -->
Files from directory 'conf_templates' should be copied to directory 'conf' with jinja2 filters applied

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
Now I can fetch those files to my local machine into temporary directory and then copy them back as templates, or use 'replace' module to replace jinja2 placeholders.

title: Add support for subuid and subgid to user module
body:
<!--- Verify first that your feature was not already discussed on GitHub -->
<!--- Complete *all* sections as described, this form is processed automatically -->

##### SUMMARY
Subuid's and subgid's are used as part of Linux user namespaces. Heavily relied on by container technologies such as podman/docker.

Adding subuid and subgid support in the user module would make this easier.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
The ansible user module

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
The usermod command supports the following:
```
usermod --add-subuids 200000-201000 --add-subgids 200000-201000 johndoe
```

This results in a corresponding entry in `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid`
```
johndoe:200000:1000
```
<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->

It could possibly look, like either this:
```yaml
- name: Add the user 'johndoe' with a specific uid and a primary group of 'admin'
  user:
    name: johndoe
    comment: John Doe
    uid: 1001
    group: admin
    subuids: 200000-201000
    subgids: 200000-201000
```
or this:
```yaml
- name: Add the user 'johndoe' with a specific uid and a primary group of 'admin'
  user:
    name: johndoe
    comment: John Doe
    uid: 1001
    group: admin
    subuid: 200000:1000
    subgid: 200000:1000
```
Depending on why you're using it, there may need to be a handler run to execute some commands, similar to the systemd daemon needing a reload after modifying a service file.  I'm just not 100% sure on what all the possible combinations of a user task being "changed" are that would require this handler to be triggered.  IE, updating a comment or group would not.

<!--- HINT: You can also paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

title: Feature request: provide capability to combine tags using logic AND or OR conditions
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
core

##### SUMMARY
 As an ansible user I can use tags to selectively execute tasks. If I specify several tags in the same command, then ansible will execute all tasks which have at least one tag from the list specified by command. I.e. it performs logical disjunction on tags set. However we ran across multiple scenarios when we need ability to perform logical conjunction on list of tags to determine list of tasks to execute. 
For example: we defined set of tags representing type operation: "install", "restart"; and another set of tags representing application type: "server", "agent", "broker". Now when I want to perform limit my activity by performing only 'restart' restart for 'server' and 'agent' , I would run my playbook with syntax like that: 
    ansible-playbook my-playbook.yml --tags="(server || agent) && restart"

title: ansible.cfg lookup paths for ansible-playbook
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ansible.cfg

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
N/A

##### SUMMARY
Would ya'll be open to a PR that adds a new location for finding `ansible.cfg`?
I would like for `ansible-playbook` to search sibling to the playbook being used. I think it should be possible to run `ansible-playbook` from any directory     _without modifying global or per-user configuration files_ and have customized `ansible.cfg` settings respected.
I'm proposing a lookup precedence of:
- ANSIBLE_CONFIG (an environment variable)
- ansible.cfg (in the current directory)
- **ansible.cfg (sibling to the playbook when ansible-playbook is used)**
- .ansible.cfg (in the home directory)\n- /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg
I understand this wouldn't make sense for the `ansible` command, but I'm hopeful that isn't considered an issue."

title: Allow to customize tmp directory and file names for template validation
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

All file modules (template, lineinfile, etc)

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
ansible 2.2.0.0
  config file = /path/to/application/playbooks/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```

##### CONFIGURATION

N/A (not related, but yes I have changed it)

```
[defaults]
hash_behaviour = merge
```

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

N/A

##### SUMMARY

Sometimes validators look at given filenames and behaves depending on it, so default naming of tmp files breaks them.

For example, next task will not validate:

```yaml
- name: 'SuperApp Systemd configuration'
  template:
    src:  superapp.service
    dest: /etc/systemd/system/superapp.service
    validate: 'systemd-analyze verify %s'
  become: yes
```

Because `systemd-analyze verify` will look to the passed filename and will expect that it will be in `name.unittype` format, so it can guess what it is (service or socket or mount…) and validate it appropriately. But Ansible pass it a tmp filename like `/home/user/.ansible/tmp/ansible-tmp-1481553589.23-30890879795771/source` and it fails.  Also it will try to load any referenced units and validate against them (and if we create them in the same ansible-playbook run it will be very helpful to have them in the same tmp directory with the same names).

See the man for more info: https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd-analyze.html

##### FEATURE IDEA

Allow to specify in playbooks:

 1. That tmp filename should be the same as target filename `/home/user/.ansible/tmp/ansible-tmp-1481553589.23-30890879795771/superapp.service` for example above.

 2. That for some files (for example in the same task with `with_items`) the same tmp directory should be used.

##### RELATED ISSUES

 1. https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/19243

title: Appending or "stacking" variable lists
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
- Feature Idea
##### COMPONENT NAME

<!--- Name of the plugin/task/feature -->

Ansible support for appending or "stacking" Variable Lists

In this case, with "with_items"
--Should be able to append variable lists on the fly, conditionally
##### ANSIBLE VERSION

<!--- Paste verbatim output from “ansible --version” between quotes below -->

```
ansible --version
ansible 2.1.0.0
  config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides

```
##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

<!---
N/A
-->
##### SUMMARY

<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->

We have the goal of dynamically being able to append lists to another list in a variable during a task. This setup is for the configuration of sec on rhel 6. We have different variables we want appended to a base list, based on conditions being met by the host.

For example, we have three variables defined at the moment. One is listed in /defaults/main.yml. A basic list, then two others that correspond to additional configurations needed by different types of application hosts.

Let's call the vars:
base
app1 
app2

Pure python can handle recursion. If we set:

```
  base = base + app1
  then set: base = base + app2
  we get: base = base + app1 + app2
```

But Ansible does not appear to support this. It fails with a, "recursion detected in template..." error. If you fix the self-reference, Ansible  of course will only accepts the last value set, for example:

```
  base = config + app1
  then set: base = config + app2
  we get: base = config + app2
```

I've seen the same error pretty much spelled out here: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/ansible-project/Jo50nu3g2b4

As of right now, we can get around this by listing all the variables (and only including the variables based on conditions being met by the host). For example:

```
- name: write /etc/sysconfig/sec 
  lineinfile: 
    state: present 
    dest: /path/to/file
    backup: yes 
    insertafter: EOF 
    line: "{{ item }}" 
  with_items: 
    - "{{ base }}"
    - "{{ app1 | default(omit) }}"
    - "{{ app2 | default(omit) }}"
```

We are required to continue appending variables under with_items, since currently, Ansible only uses the last definition of the variable.

But it would be nice to have ansible be able to handle appending variable values, so we could simply include something akin to the following in vars/app1.yml:

```

---
app1:  <value>
base: "{{ base + app1 }}"

```

Which would make it easier to manage large numbers of hosts which may need various mixes of settings applied. Including recursion in the variable file would cover all cases (appending the needed value to the base variable on the fly), instead of having to create the var.yml file and then still go back in and add the "{{ app# | default(omit) }}" to the list.....

title: ansible-galaxy should download dependencies in meta/main.yml
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ansible-galaxy

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
Future

##### CONFIGURATION
N/A

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY
Local roles may depend on galaxy roles defined in `meta/main.yml`. `ansible-galaxy install -r` does not support this file format, although the value of the `dependencies` key appears to match that in a `requirements.yml` file

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

```
$ cat meta/main.yml

galaxy_info:
  author: ome-devel@lists.openmicroscopy.org.uk
dependencies:
- role: openmicroscopy.basedeps
```

```
$ ansible-galaxy install -r meta/main.yml`

- downloading role 'dependencies', owned by 
 [WARNING]: - dependencies was NOT installed successfully: Role has no field
named u'owner'

ERROR! - you can use --ignore-errors to skip failed roles and finish processing the list.
```
Proposed behaviour: Install all roles listed in the `dependencies` section of `meta/main.yml`, in this case `openmicroscopy.basedeps`

##### BACKGROUND

- [Galaxy dependencies file can be the role meta file](https://github.com/metacloud/molecule/pull/717#issuecomment-273455237)
  This is the original motivation for this feature suggestion. When using [molecule](https://github.com/metacloud/molecule) for testing Ansible roles dependencies need to be in a requirements file. If this is a deliberate design decision it would be useful if the docs could be updated with an official description of the `meta/main.yml` file.
- [Add ability to automatically download roles when running play](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/12927)
  This is a closely related feature request.

title: Support for Logon to Amazon EC2 Container Registry
body:
From @osiegmar on 2016-10-16T15:34:56Z
##### ISSUE TYPE

<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
- Feature Idea
##### COMPONENT NAME

docker_login
##### ANSIBLE VERSION

<!--- Paste verbatim output from “ansible --version” between quotes below -->

```
2.1.1.0
```
##### SUMMARY

<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->

As far as I know, the only way to logon to (and pull docker images from / push to) [Amazon ECR](https://aws.amazon.com/de/ecr/) is via the shell module. It would be nice if the docker_login module supports logon to ECR so that further ansible docker tasks can directly work with ECR.

This is how I handle this currently:

```
- name: ECR login
  shell: "$(aws ecr get-login --region eu-central-1)"
- name: Pull image from ECR
  shell: "docker pull myid.dkr.ecr.eu-central-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:latest"
```

The output from `aws ecr get-login` is:

```
docker login -u AWS -p [VERY-LONG-PASSWORD] -e none https://myid.dkr.ecr.eu-central-1.amazonaws.com
```

The generated password is valid for 12 hours.

Of course, I could use awk to get the password from the output and then use docker_login. But the output format may change...

Copied from original issue: ansible/ansible-modules-core#5277

title: make git module support owner and group parameters
body:
##### SUMMARY
make `git module` to support both `owner` and `group` parameters like copy module

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
git

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
In case that we want to clone a git repository under a specific user (except for root and default user)  ownership, `become: (yes|no)` won't work well because it just decides which user, root or default user, should own the directory created with git module.

Even if we intend to use the directory with the user, it ends up with seeing the message like  "Permission denied .git/FETCH_HEAD".

It can be achieved by using `file module` after `git module`, but it's a little bit troublesome.
  

```yaml
- tasks:
  - name: cloning git with configuration of ownership on dest directory
    git: 
      repo: "ssh://git@github.com/ORG/Example.git"
      dest: "/var/www/Example"
      ssh_opts: "xxxx"
      key_file: "yyyy"
      owner: app # nice to have
      group: app #  nice to have
```

title: add dnf module enable/disable support

##### SUMMARY
Update the dnf module to enable/disable modules (currently only install/remove is supported). 

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
- dnf

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
To install 389-ds-base on CentOS 8, you first need to enable module '389-ds:1.4'. 
Using `dnf: name="@389-ds" state=present`, the module will not be enabled. 

Latest version of python-dnf does support enabling modules:
```import dnf

base = dnf.Base()
base.read_all_repos()
base.fill_sack()

module_base = dnf.module.module_base.ModuleBase(base)
module_base.enable(['389-ds:1.4'])

base.do_transaction()
```

title: Merge ansible config files
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea
##### COMPONENT NAME

ansible.cfg
##### SUMMARY

Merge configurations instead of using the first configuration found.

I think it would be helpful if ansible merged the configuation files instead of just using the first one it finds. This would allow to have a repo specific configurations while allowing people to keep their local settings such as forks or ssh options. Especially setting `roles_path` to `./` in a repo configs would allow big repos to place playbooks in a subdirectory while including them in site.yml in repo root. Without setting roles_path the playbooks in the sub directory can't find roles unless those are symlinked (suppose that's the same with group_vars and host_vars). Also it's not very feasible to set full paths in global or user local ansible.cfg when there are multiple ansible repos with separate roles.

title: Feature idea: within-host parallelization for with_items loops
body:
This has come up before (for example, see [this mailing list thread](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/ansible-devel/lhvukkszKUA)), but the basic idea is that you may want to run a large number of `command` module operations on a single host in parallel via `with_items` if they don't have any dependencies on one another.

In that thread, the specific example is a 10-host inventory, but with about 500 commands to run on each host, each command taking 2 seconds.

It seems that Ansible has multi-host parallelism baked into it deeply, but doing any kind of within-host parallelism requires some hackery. For example, you can trick Ansible into using it's multi-host parallelism by creating fake inventory entries and having a play that targets those "hosts", which are really all just aliases of the same machine ([as suggested here](https://groups.google.com/d/msg/ansible-project/8Yh4jy68Kmk/GJC2y44XjNAJ)). Depending on the complexity of your playbook, this might get complicated to do, and it obfuscates what's really going on for people reading the playbook.

Another thing you can do is to run a script that has some parallelism internally (for example, you can template out a script that forks and runs multiple commands) but then you lose some of the nice things that Ansible gives you around change-detection and reporting.

So, the idea here is effectively to add some kind of option to Ansible to signal that it should try running multiple entries from a `with_items` list in parallel (probably a number indicating the desired level maximum of concurrent tasks) when connecting to a particular host. I can see this as being most useful with the `command` module (or perhaps the `shell` module), but there may be other use cases as well.

title: COPR support for DNF
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
dnf

##### SUMMARY
*Copr is a Fedora community build service in Fedora that builds your open-source project and creates your own RPM repository.*

It is sort of an RPM equivalent for the Arch User Repository; although one can host binary packages there.

[Here is the DNF plugin documentation](https://dnf-plugins-core.readthedocs.io/en/latest/copr.html), please note that the copr plugin is not necessarily installed, and can be installed through `dnf-plugin(copr)`'.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
Example to enable a copr repo (eq. `dnf copr enable rubdos/linux_media`)

```yaml
- name: Enable repo
  dnf: enable_copr=rubdos/linux_media
```

Example to disable a copr repo (eq. `dnf copr disable rubdos/linux_media`)

```yaml
- name: Enable repo
  dnf: disable_copr=rubdos/linux_media
```

*note:* feel free to diverge from this syntax; perhaps something more "stateful" is useful here?

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
Respectively the equivalent of  `dnf copr enable rubdos/linux_media` and `dnf copr disable rubdos/linux_media`.

title: iptables: option to run iptables-save
body:
_From @mrooney on September 28, 2016 19:10_

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea
##### COMPONENT NAME

iptables module
##### ANSIBLE VERSION

ansible 2.1.0.0
##### SUMMARY

As documented, "This module does not handle the saving and/or loading of rules, but rather only manipulates the current rules that are present in memory". However, it would be awesome if there was an optional "save" option which default to false, that did run iptables-save for you.

Motivation:
1. this would be consistent with the `mount` core module which does not just run the mount commands but also allows persisting of them to the fstab.
2. it would be super nice to simply do:

```
- iptables: table=nat chain=PREROUTING protocol=tcp match=tcp destination_port=80 jump=REDIRECT to_ports=8080 save=true
  become: yes
```

instead of presently:

```
- iptables: table=nat chain=PREROUTING protocol=tcp match=tcp destination_port=80 jump=REDIRECT to_ports=8080
  become: yes
  register: port_forward
- name: save iptables
  command: iptables-save
  become: true
  when: port_forward|changed
```

Thanks for the excellent module and the consideration!

title: Module "skip" to skip remaining play
body:
I would propose a module "skip" to skip the remaining play.

Usage would be something like:

``` yaml
- action: /opt/proprietary/application --version
  register: app_version

- action: skip
  only_if: '${app_version.stdout}' != '1.2.3'

- action: command mktemp -d
  register: tempdir
- action: command rsync -aH /share/software/proprietary/application-1.2.3 ${tempdir.stdout}
- action: command ${tempdir.stdout}/ugly-stuff --to --do --manual --install
```

Another way to do this could be:

``` yaml
- action: /opt/proprietary/application --version
  register: app_version
  skip_if: '${app_version}' != '1.2.3'
```

not sure if we want to enlarge the list of custom task attributes this way...

title: Allow roles to depend on collections
body:
##### SUMMARY

I have a number of roles currently on Ansible Galaxy, which I will likely continue to support as standalone roles for a time (and not part of a collection).

Many of those roles depend on modules which may, at some point, be maintained in a collection outside of ansible/ansible, meaning my role would require the collection be installed and loaded in order to use the module.

Currently I can have my role `meta/main.yml` file list `dependencies` (which is a list of roles it depends on), and I can be sure the dependencies are run before my role using that mechanism.

I would like to be able to say "my `geerlingguy.x` role requires collection `somethingelse.y`", and then Ansible would ensure that `somethingelse.y` collection is available, just as if the playbook itself had added:

```yaml
  collections:
    - somethingelse.y
```

Real-world example:

  - `geerlingguy.mysql` (existing role) uses the `mysql_users` and `mysql_db` modules, which are _currently_ part of Ansible core.
  - `mysql_users` and `mysql_db` are both listed as community modules
  - At some point, these modules will likely end up in `ansible.community` or some other community collection.
  - When that happens, do I need to tell all my role users to manually:
     1. `ansible-galaxy collection install ansible.community`
     2. Update playbook to add `collections: ['ansible.community']`

I would prefer to not have to ask all the role users to do that—but that is really in one sense two different feature requests:

  1. Installing community collections automatically (whether in some role's requirements, or maybe if it's listed in a playbook's `collections` list)
  2. Allowing roles' requirements to autoload a collection in a given playbook run.

##### ISSUE TYPE

- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

roles
collections

N/A

title: Provide a way to run some tasks after failure, at the playbook level
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Playbooks

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
ansible 2.1.0.0
  config file = /home/rene/.ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```
##### SUMMARY

I use Ansible for application deployments. I want to make sure that only one deployment, or only one deployment per project, runs against a given server at a time, to avoid race conditions, problems with apt locking, and so on. So at a certain point in my playbook, I would like to create a lockfile. I would then like to remove that lockfile at the end of the playbook, no matter if there was an error or not. `block:` / `always:` exists, but it is not quite flexible enough to write this in a nice way, because a block needs to be insisde a `tasks:` item. Here is an example of a syntax style I would like to have:

```
- hosts: appservers
  roles:
  - appserver
  - another_role
  pre_tasks:
  - name: Wait for lockfile
    wait_for:
      path: /tmp/deployment.{{ project_name }}.lock
      state: absent
  - name: Write lockfile
    command: lockfile /tmp/deployment.{{ project_name }}.lock
  post_tasks:
  - name: Remove lockfile
    file:
      path: /tmp/deployment.{{ project_name }}.lock
      state: absent
    ***run_always: true***
```

At the moment, I have to remove the lockfile manually after a failure. It would be nice if I could handle this from my playbook.

title: Live output from remote processes
body:
Hi there,

we're currently test driving some of our old scripts remotely from ansible and if we choose to use it probably will for some time.

On the one hand ansible makes it really easy to automate workflows that span multiple servers (that we'd like to use) on the other hand though it makes it really hard to see live whats going on on the other system.

What we would like is a way to see the output of the task on the target system as live feedback (possibly if requested via an option in a playbook).

As far as I can see that is not possible with the current design of playbooks as the underlying callback object does not get callbacks for individual lines in the playbook.

As far as I understand how ansible works (ssh into the other machine run a command) this should not be difficult to implement as the output is streamed live to the local machine anyway (or could be easily).

So that's the feature request: show live output of remote tasks/commands. How about it? Would you be willing to do that in ansible? Would you accept a pull request? Is it already possible?

Best regards and thanks for the great tool!

title: Allow creation/deletion of chains the 'iptables' module
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
iptables

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.3.0.0 (stable-2.3 4b5553c97f) last updated 2017/04/23 13:15:41 (GMT +200)
  config file =
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
  python version = 2.7.9 (default, Mar  1 2015, 12:57:24) [GCC 4.9.2]
```

##### CONFIGURATION
N/A

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY
I propose to extend the iptables module to allow creation and deletion of chains. The following are samples of the commands that should be supported for both ipv4 and ipv6 (the `ip_version` is omitted for simplicity). The `table` parameter is optional and default to `filter`.

* deleting a single chain
  ```
  iptables [-t mytable] -X mychain
  ```
  would become
  ```
  iptables:
    chain: mychain
    table: mytable
    state: absent
  ```
* deleting all chains
  ```
  iptables [-t mytable] -X
  ```
  would become
  ```
  iptables:
    table: mytable
    state: absent
  ```
* adding a chain
  ```
  iptables [-t mytable] -N mychain
  ```
  would become
  ```
  iptables:
    chain: mychain
    table: mytable
    state: present
  ```

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
N/A

N/A

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
N/A

title: Helm module replacement
body:
##### SUMMARY
This is a proposal to a new helm module

Why a new module:
- security improvement: In order to use the current helm module you need to expose your tiller to the outside world, which is both insecure and completely unnecessary: #32513
- Current helm module is not compatible with helm 3 (already in beta) by design because it doesn't work without tiller which has been removed in helm 3
- Maintaner says `not been able to maintain it due to a change of focus and lack of time`: https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/37148#issuecomment-441284531 on november 2018

What we proposed:
A new helm module based on helm cli like terraform module
You can find implementation here: #62450

body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - ~Bug  Report~
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
core

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from "ansible --version" between quotes below -->
```
2.4.3
```

##### CONFIGURATION
<!---
If using Ansible 2.4 or above, paste the results of "ansible-config dump --only-changed"
Otherwise, mention any settings you have changed/added/removed in ansible.cfg
(or using the ANSIBLE_* environment variables).
-->

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY
While it is possible to tell ansible to log to file using the 

At this moment there are 3 variables/options that do affect the output behaviour:
* ``ANSIBLE_STDOUT_CALLBACK``
* ``ANSIBLE_VERBOSITY``
* ``ANSIBLE_DEBUG``

The most important problem highlighted by this bug is the **impossibility to record extra verbosity inside log files while having  brief console output**. 

This bug highly affects continuous integration systems using medium or bigger Ansible playbooks where the CI system performance is negatively affected by too verbose console output (Jenkins being well known for this, but also Travis being affected). On the other hand the users need extra verbosity from time to time in order to be able to identify exactly what went wrong. Now they cannot have both.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

I created a [small repository on github](https://github.com/ssbarnea/ansible-output) which has only one purpose: to capture ansible output with different callbacks and verbosity levels, so we can easily compare the results.

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
Be able to use different verbosity levels for console and for file logging, eventually even different logging callbacks.


##### ACTUAL RESULTS
Ansible file logging is always based on console logging, the only difference being the prefix added to each printed line. 

Still, when it comes to formatting rules or verbosity levels there is no control.

title: Ansible does not expose current home directory of the remote user
body:
- Bug Report
##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
ansible 2.0.2.0
  config file =
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```
##### SUMMARY

It seems that at this moment Ansible does not expose the home directory of the remote use. When I say remote user, I mean that user on which the command is supposed to run under. When become_user=www-data, I would expect to have a variable that resolves as the home directory of this user.

The problem is well described at http://stackoverflow.com/questions/33343215/how-to-get-remote-users-home-directory-in-ansible and there is a workaround which is very-very ugly and hard to use.

We need a native way to do this, one that does not force us to create additional tasks for loading this value.

title: Allow digital signature verification in get_url
body:
### SUMMARY
The `get_url` module does file integrity checking and allows passing a SHASUM text file on a remote host over HTTP.  Typically, binaries and their hashes are stored together so while the integrity control works there is no verification that the binary and its corresponding SHASUM file has not be altered by a malicious actor.  Sometimes software providers will include a digital signature for cryptographically verifying the SHASUM file.  This is a feature request to support verifying digital signatures.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
get_url

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
Consider the software package Vault by HashiCorp.  It's a single binary in a zip archive precompiled for practically every common architecture and operating system.  It's used to protect secrets, so there is value in a threat actor modifying the code to install backdoors or other code to compromise or disclose those secrets.

HashiCorp is aware of this so they have a GPG key pair where the private key they keep secret and the public key they publish on their website and in all major GPG public key servers.  Whenever they create a new release, they SHA256 all of the binaries, put them in a SHA256SUMS file, and sign it with their private key.

I would imagine providing an attribute for `get_url` to specify the public key ID as well as the path to the digital signature.  For example:

```yaml
- name: Download vault
  get_url:
    url: https://releases.hashicorp.com/vault/1.4.1/vault_1.4.1_linux_arm64.zip
    dest: vault_1.4.1_linux_arm64.zip
    checksum: sha256:https://releases.hashicorp.com/vault/1.4.1/vault_1.4.1_SHA256SUMS
    checksum_sig: 51852D87348FFC4C:https://releases.hashicorp.com/vault/1.4.1/vault_1.4.1_SHA256SUMS.sig
```

Right now I have to download the SHA256SUMS file to verify the signature, and then I can't pass that file to the get_url checksum parameter because it only accepts the raw checksum or a URL; it would also be nice to pass a local path to this field and the hypothesized checksum_sig field.

These are the steps I am using to ensure the integrity and authenticity of Vault:

```shell
# Import HashiCorp's Public Key from the OpenGPG Public Key Server
$ gpg --keyserver keys.openpgp.org --recv-keys 51852D87348FFC4C
gpg: directory '/root/.gnupg' created
gpg: keybox '/root/.gnupg/pubring.kbx' created
gpg: /root/.gnupg/trustdb.gpg: trustdb created
gpg: key 51852D87348FFC4C: public key "HashiCorp Security <security@hashicorp.com>" imported
gpg: Total number processed: 1
gpg:               imported: 1

# Download all files
$ curl -Os https://releases.hashicorp.com/vault/1.4.1/vault_1.4.1_SHA256SUMS
$ curl -Os https://releases.hashicorp.com/vault/1.4.1/vault_1.4.1_SHA256SUMS.sig
$ curl -Os https://releases.hashicorp.com/vault/1.4.1/vault_1.4.1_linux_arm64.zip

# Verify signature
$ gpg --verify vault_1.4.1_SHA256SUMS.sig vault_1.4.1_SHA256SUMS
gpg: Signature made Thu Apr 30 08:20:29 2020

title: Pass filenames to `values` option of `helm` module
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

[`helm` module](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/2.4/helm_module.html)

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
ansible 2.4.0.0
  config file = None
  configured module search path = [u'/Users/jeremie/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /usr/local/Cellar/ansible/2.4.0.0/libexec/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible
  executable location = /usr/local/bin/ansible
  python version = 2.7.14 (default, Sep 25 2017, 09:53:17) [GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 8.0.0 (clang-800.0.42.1)]
```

##### CONFIGURATION

None

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

Mac OSX El Capitan 10.11.6

##### SUMMARY

`helm install` takes a YAML file as its `--values` argument. From `helm help install`:

```
 -f, --values valueFiles      specify values in a YAML file (can specify multiple) (default [])
```

It would be very useful if that file could be provided as an argument of the `helm` module.
For example, it could help transitioning from `command: helm install --values my-values.yml` to the Ansible module while keeping one's values file separate instead embedded in the tasks.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

Syntax could look as follows. If `values` is a string, treat it as a filename (regular lookup in `files` directory of the role):

```yaml
- helm:
    values: my-values.yml
```

`helm install` also supports multiple files, so that could be:

```yaml
- helm:
    values:
      - my-values1.yml
      - my-values2.yml
```

If a dict is passed, keep the current behavior.

If it is not acceptable to have `values` do both, a separate `value_file(s)` argument would do the trick.

title: Proper reboot module as action plugin
body:
<!--- Verify first that your issue/request is not already reported in GitHub -->
##### ISSUE TYPE

<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
reboot

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
v2.5

##### SUMMARY

<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->

It would be very useful if Ansible shipped a proper `reboot` module that works for all operating systems. If you see what people have to do today to reboot systems it is clear a better implementation (that works for every operating system) is in order.

The action-plugin would have the following options:
- A delay to give some time to the user(s) to act
- A timeout to ensure that if the reboot has failed Ansible escalates (timeout does not include delay-time)
- A notification message for the user(s)
- Possibly regathering the system facts after the reboot
- A proper check to see if the system has in fact rebooted (check uptime fact)

Various issues have been reported wrt. reboot functionality:
- [If managed system reboots, shell module does not return rc value](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/15741)
- [Reboot and Wait for](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/14413)
- [wait_for: Python exception "socket.error" thrown with search_regex](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/14675)
- [play stopped after reboot in RHEL/CentOS 7](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/9632)
- [Issuing reboot command with sudo fails](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/7124)
- [System reboot module](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/5785)

And various articles have been written regarding the catches, each with their own tricks/advice:
- [Reboot a server and wait for it to come back](https://support.ansible.com/hc/en-us/articles/201958037-Reboot-a-server-and-wait-for-it-to-come-back)
- [How to wait for server restart using Ansible?](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/23877781/how-to-wait-for-server-restart-using-ansible)
- [Ansible: Reboot server in play-book and wait for it come back.](https://www.linux.com/blog/ansible-reboot-server-play-book-and-wait-it-come-back)
- [Rebooting server(s) using Ansible](https://oguya.ch/posts/2015-02-22-ansible-reboot-servers/)
- [Using Ansible to Reboot or Shut Down Devices Running Junos OS](http://www.juniper.net/techpubs/en_US/junos-ansible1.0/topics/example/junos-ansible-playbooks-device-rebooting.html)
- [Rebooting Servers One-by-One With Ansible](http://elnur.pro/rebooting-servers-one-by-one-with-ansible/)

title: feature: controlling ignore-errors output
body:
#### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ignore_errors

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
N/A

##### SUMMARY

When running with `ignore_errors: true`, ansible by defaults outputs the red-failure and ...ignoring.
This can be a little problematic when running big plays, because the failed/red text is scaring, and
causes scrolling in history to see exactly what went wrong (nothing) ;)

Also, `failed_when: false` is not really usable, since one can not use: `result|failed`

I suggest adding something like the following:

```
- name: check that port is listening
  wait_for: port=80 state=started timeout=10
  register: _listening
  ignore_errors: true
    text: "port 80 is not open"
    text_style: ok
```

..or just similar whatever with similar effect:
- keep the "real" failed/success status in the registered variable
- keep ansible from outputting this as a error (even if ignoring)

title: Hosts ranges for limit option
body:
##### SUMMARY

Provide opportunity for iterate hosts in `--limit` option same way as it is realized for inventory files
Examples:

```
ansible -m ping 'node[7:11]'
ansible -m ping 'db-[a:f].example.com'
ansible -m ping 'www[01:50].example.com'
```
Regexp matching is not solves problem at all, this is really useless,
eg. how to select nodes from `node7` to `node11`:

```
ansible -m ping 'node([7-9]|1[0:1])'
```
Good solution is proposed kubernetes guys, kubelet just uses built in shell iterators:

```
kubectl label node node{7..11} my-label=
```
But ansible supports only lists separated by comma.
This really gives pain to do the next thing any time:

```
ansible -m ping $(echo node{7..11} | tr ' ' ',')
```
There is also old feature request https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/4620, which still have many empathizing persons, but it was closed without reason. And no one don't wont to open it again. (I was ask really).
So I've created new one. I hope this peoples will support me.

title: RFE: Option to ignore whitespace difference in --diff mode
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
core

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
any

##### SUMMARY
We would like to be able to have `--diff` mode support ignoring whitespace changes. This would help from time to time to see the true differences between certain configuration files that are being pushed (and of which we have no control over).

Additionally, it should ignore newline differences ('\r\n' vs '\n') because we have cases where the diff does not have any visible changes at all.

This is similar to `--diff-ignore-space-changes`.

title: Rename synchronize module owner/group to preserve_owner/preserve_group
body:
**Issue Type**: Bug Report
**Ansible Version**: 1.9.4
##### Summary

I think the synchronize module owner & group options are currently confusing and would better be renamed to preserve_owner and preserve_group.

Reasons:
- Both options in all other modules accept username/group, while in synchronize module they accept yes/no
- The use case for synchronize makes it valid to think that by specifing owner & group options we are asking it to change ownership on destination, not preserve them as is
- Backwards-compatibility concerns aside, it might be worth considering re-introducing owner and group options but for chown-ing files on dest (similar to rsync's --chown) which IMHO is more useful in ansible than preserving ownership since files are usually maintained in a VCS and have no guaranteed ownership

title: Add limit of number of backup files to file modules with backup option
body:
From @selivan on 2016-09-21T11:03:38Z
##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea
##### COMPONENT NAME

File modules with `backup` option: `copy`, `template`, `lineinfile`, `ini_file`, `replace`.
##### SUMMARY

If you are using `backup` option for a long time, large number of backup files is piled up in config directory:

```
service.conf
service.conf.2016-03-09@12:20:22
service.conf.2016-03-15@18:17:20~
service.conf.2016-03-21@17:59:52~
service.conf.2016-03-24@19:19:26~
...
tons and tons and tons of backup files here
...
```

In my use case backup files are used to be able to quick-revert manually, if something got wrong. So old files are not interesting, they just become obsolete garbage. It would be very convenient to have options `backup_max_age` and `backup_max_files` , that will automatically clean up old backup files based on their age(in days) or total number.
##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

Something like that:

``` yaml
- name: install service config
  template: src=service.cfg dest=/etc/service/service.cfg mode=0644 backup=yes backup_max_age=14
```

If service.cfg was changed - creates new backup file and clears up backup files older than 2 weeks.

Copied from original issue: ansible/ansible-modules-core#4954
