title: Add forks per play or per task
body:
<!---
Verify first that your issue/request is not already reported on GitHub.
Also test if the latest release, and master branch are affected too.
-->

##### ISSUE TYPE
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Name of the module/plugin/task/feature -->
core

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from “ansible --version” between quotes below -->
v2.4

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->
We have the need to limit the number of tasks run in parallel for a specific task in order to not overload some infrastructure (in this case a terminal server). The total number of connections should not exceed 4.

Now we are using `serial:` for this, however this will do them 4 at a time, and it waits for the slowest one to be finished to do the next batch of 4. Which is suboptimal and slower, than using something like `forks: 4` where it fires off a new task as soon as one has finished.

For us this would make a lot of difference.

title: Make it possible to pass *command line* arguments to dynamic inventory scripts
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

Inventory

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

All of them that support dynamic inventory.

##### CONFIGURATION

N/A

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

N/A

##### SUMMARY

It doesn't appear the you can pass command line arguments to dynamic inventory, but instead must kluge together `ENV` variables or various other passing techniques (ie, write out a ini file) to side-step the inability of a dynamic inventory script to get passed a custom set of arguments; this seems sorta weird/not ideal (especially if you are using the dynamic inventory script in other programs).

For example a script we have can do the following:

`./render_env -e envs/dev json` this will output a json format of the envs topology and settings.

The same program can output yaml, or puppet renderings and it logically makes sense that this program should be able to be ran like:

`./render_env -e envs/dev ansible` and then the same program will then be able to be used with the ansible dynamic inventory routines, but we can't do that currently because we can't pass in `-e envs/dev ansible` or any other arguments, which is sorta a PITA.


##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

Try to pass a set of additional arguments to a dynamic inventory script, it won't work.

N/A

N/A

##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Bug Report

Command line interface

```

##### SUMMARY
Improve error message when key doesn't match the one in known_hosts

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
Connect to any server with an incorrect SSH key. This could happen in many ways. I my case I was rebuilding a vagrant box using ansible when I got this message.

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

Fatal error: Host key for 127.0.0.1 did not match pre-existing key! Server's key was changed recently, or possible man-in-the-middle attack.

Underlying exception:
    ('127.0.0.1', <paramiko.rsakey.RSAKey object at 0x101f2c990>, <paramiko.rsakey.RSAKey object at 0x101f48e90>)

I think you could do even better than that though: Give the two possible fixes that the user can choose from:

* Remove the old signature from ~/.ssh/known_hosts

title: Support AWS named profiles as --profile from ~/.aws/credentials file
body:
When you have multiple named profiles in your AWS credentials file on your local machine, it would be nice to have the ability to specify which profile you would like to run a playbook under.

For example:
`ansible-playbook -i ec2.py --profile production playbook.yml`

My `~/.aws/credentials` file would look like this:

```
[default]
aws_access_key_id=AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
aws_secret_access_key=wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY

[production]
aws_access_key_id=AKIAI44QH8DHBEXAMPLE
aws_secret_access_key=je7MtGbClwBF/2Zp9Utk/h3yCo8nvbEXAMPLEKEY

```

If you do not specify a `--profile` then use the default as it does in current behavior.

title: add hosts directive for import_playbook
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
import_playbook

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.4.0.0
```

##### CONFIGURATION
n/a

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
n/a

##### SUMMARY
Large environments may have a playbook for each major part of the environment, each which has multiple plays in-line. In many cases it's desirable to have a single master playbook that includes the other multi-play playbooks. This is possible with `import_playbook`, however every play will still be parsed, which can take a long time and produce a lot of useless output in complex environments.

If `import_playbook` had a `hosts` directive, or some other conditional that only applies to the import statement (instead of simply being passed to every child play), we could have much faster and cleaner execution from a single master playbook in complex environments.

Consider a simplified example with two applications, each which has a multi-tier environment with many hosts and hostgroups, plus some common infrastructure...

Directory structure:
```
ansible-repo/
    inventory
    master.yml
    playbooks/
        appA.yml
        appB.yml
        ops.yml
```

Inventory file (partial--assume appB is similar to appA):
```
[appA:children]
appA_web
appA_db

[appA_web]
appA_web[0-3]

[appA_db]
appA_db[1-2]
appA_db_readonly

[ops]
logs
monitoring

[web:children]
appA_web
appB_web

[db:children]
appA_db
appB_db
```

Each application playbook contains multiple plays (at least one for each hostgroup), and each play consists of many tasks. The master playbook contains some common tasks needed by all hosts, and it imports the other playbooks:

```
---
- hosts: all
  tasks: <common-starting-stuff>

- import_playbook: playbooks/appA.yml
- import_playbook: playbooks/appB.yml
- import_playbook: playbooks/ops.yml

- hosts: all
  tasks <common-ending-stuff>
```

For the sake of brevity this example is greatly simplified, but assume the common plays consist of many tasks and roles, and each application playbook contains tens or hundreds of in-line plays. To accomplish a simple change on a small subset of hosts requires Ansible to parse every play in all 4 playbooks.

As a workaround I have pulled common plays into their own file, which is imported at the beginning of each application playbook. The downside to this approach is that common plays are repeated from every playbook imported by master, even if they only need to run once.

If `import_playbook` offered a `hosts` directive, or anything similar, we could retain the simplicity of a single master playbook that only runs the relevant imported plays:
```
---
- hosts: all
  tasks: <common-starting-stuff>

- import_playbook: playbooks/appA.yml
  hosts: appA

- import_playbook: playbooks/appB.yml
  hosts: appB

- import_playbook: playbooks/ops.yml
  hosts: ops

- hosts: all
  tasks <common-ending-stuff>
```

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
n/a

title: [Systemd] Allow ansible to update the default systemd target mode
body:
<!--- Verify first that your feature was not already discussed on GitHub -->
<!--- Complete *all* sections as described, this form is processed automatically -->

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Describe the new feature/improvement briefly below -->
In some case it can be useful to update the default target level for systemd.
This is used from the "get-default", "set-default" and "isolate" systemd options.
- The "get-default" option will retrieve the current default target mode.
- The "set-default" option will set a new default target mode (used on next boot)
- The"isolate" option will set the new target mode dynamically

A combination of these 3 options will allow to manage the target mode in systemd

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Write the short name of the module, plugin, task or feature below, use your best guess if unsure -->
This will be a new functionality in the systemd.py module.

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
<!--- Describe how the feature would be used, why it is needed and what it would solve -->
This feature will required 2 options:
- default_set
       description:
            - Systemd default target you want to apply on your system
              Target modes can be included and actives in others. Such as "multi-user.target" which is included and active in "graphical.target" mode.
              If you want to ensure that you are running in the desired mode, you can use the "default_apply": "enforce" option to force the mode.
- default_apply:
        description: Apply the Systemd default target if required.
              * The 'no' option will set the desired target mode for next boot. It will not update the current node.
              * The 'yes' option will set the desired target mode and ensure that the desired mode is active.
              * The 'only' option will only ensure that the desired mode is active.
              * The 'enforce' option will do the same stuff as 'yes' but it will enforce the mode at the desired level.
                This can be used to change from "graphical.target" to "multi-user.target" and stop the graphical mode.
                This should be used only once otherwise the task will always be seen as changed.
        choices: [ no, yes, only, enforce ]


<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
- name: set the default target to "graphical.target", it will be applied on next boot (2.10 and above)
  systemd:
   default_set: graphical.target

- name: apply the target mode "multi-user.target".
  systemd:
    default_set: multi-user.target
    default_apply: only
```

<!--- HINT: You can also paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

title: Add 'allow_change_held_packages' option to apt module.
body:
<!--- Verify first that your feature was not already discussed on GitHub -->
<!--- Complete *all* sections as described, this form is processed automatically -->

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Describe the new feature/improvement briefly below -->
When installing or updating an already held apt package with the `apt` module, it fails. 
This forces to use the `force: yes` option that it's too broad and already deprecated by apt.

Add an option `allow_change_held_packages` to the `apt` module to use the `--allow-change-held-packages` `apt` option.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Write the short name of the module, plugin, task or feature below, use your best guess if unsure -->
apt

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
<!--- Describe how the feature would be used, why it is needed and what it would solve -->


<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
- name: Install package with specific version and hold
  block:
    - name: Install {{ package_name }} version {{ version }}"
      apt:
        name: "{{ package_name }}={{ version }}"
        state: present
        (force|allow_change_held_packages): yes

    - name: Hold {{ package_name }} package
      dpkg_selections:
        name: "{{ package_name }}"
        selection: hold
```

This is not idempotent without the `force: yes`, the second run will fail. 
The `allow_change_held_packages` will avoid problems with this special situation.

<!--- HINT: You can also paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->


Related to: https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/15394

title: include_vars is unable to load files from remote
body:
It seems that `include_vars` cannot be used to load files from remote systems and that is not documented https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/modules/include_vars_module.html

1. Documentation should clarify that include_vars works only on controler and cannot be delagted.

2. User should get an error or at least a warning when using ``delegate_to`` with ``include_vars``, current Ansible does neither of them.

3. When file/dir cannot be found Ansible gives a disinformative error message which suggests that user may want to use `remote_src `option, an option that does not exist on ``include_vars``.

>  "message": "Could not find or access '/etc/docker/config.json' on the Ansible Controller.\nIf you are using a module and expect the file to exist on the remote, see the remote_src option"

Ideally include_vars should be allowed to run on remote hosts because it would allow easy loading of configuration files from various YAML or JSON files, [somethign that currently is a real PITA](https://stackoverflow.com/a/41654953/99834) to do (both shell-cat and slurp-decode are ugly and overcomplicated).

The new EC2 Launch Template feature will be used everywhere where ec2_lc can be currently used, i.e. to setup launch settings of ASG. Launch Templates support setting of AWS T2 Unlimited flag, which is particularly important advantage of Launch Templates over Launch Configurations. Furthermore, it can be used to launch EC2 instances which are not part of ASG. Support of EC2 Launch Templates in Ansible via ec2_lt is useful even while other Ansible modules do not support Launch Templates yet.

Out of scope of this feature request is support of EC2 Launch Templates by other Ansible modules.

If connection was broken during download, retry download [retry]-counts. Do not download at start, resume/continue on the stopped end like 'wget -c'. Also resume or continue if playbook is restarted manually after end of retry count or any playbook interrupt. If anything after the download fails like not enough memory or permission problems, keep the temporary file. Resume transfering the temporary file to the destination on the next user attempt. Only delete it, if task was completed successfully.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, show exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case.
For new features, show how the feature would be used.
-->

<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
- name: download some files
  get_url:
     url: http://example.example/file.txt
     dest: /my/downloads
     retry_limit: 3
     continue: yes
```

<!--- You can also paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
<!--- What did you expect to happen when running the steps above? -->
file is downloaded correctly, even the server times out

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
<!--- What actually happened? If possible run with extra verbosity (-vvvv) -->
server times out, file downloaded from beginning, server times out again...

title: Please autotag roles with the role name
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea
##### COMPONENT NAME

Core
##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
$ ansible --version
ansible 2.2.0 (devel 4ed88512e4) last updated 2016/09/07 08:35:44 (GMT +200)
  lib/ansible/modules/core: (detached HEAD 5a5c1491ae) last updated 2016/09/07 08:37:39 (GMT +200)
  lib/ansible/modules/extras: (detached HEAD 1474bde864) last updated 2016/09/07 08:37:39 (GMT +200)
  config file = /var/scratch/src/ansible/ansible/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides

```
##### CONFIGURATION
##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

Debian unstable
##### SUMMARY

Please autotag roles with the role name:

I always end up writing code like:

```
roles:
  - { role: webserver, tags: [ 'webserver' ] }
  - { role: proxy, tags: [ 'proxy' ] }
  - { role: foobar, tags: [ 'foobar' ] }
```

This allows to run and test roles independently (otherwise they'd had dependencies on each other anyway) like:

```
ansible-playbook -i localhost, -l localhost play.yml -t foobar
```

Now it would be nice if the all tasks in a role would automatically get tagged with the role name so this would become:

```
roles:
  - webserver
  - proxy
  - foobar
```

But I could still use

```
ansible-playbook -i localhost, -l localhost play.yml -t foobar
```

to only run tasks from the _foobar_ role.

title: RFE: permit passing stdin to commands/shells
body:
I want to be able to provide data to a command via stdin in my ansible playbooks.  For example, I have a playbook that uses `sfdisk` to handle partition manipulation, this:

```
- command: sfdisk -N2
  data: |
    label: dos
    /tmp/archlinux.img1 : start=        2048, size=      204800, type=c
    /tmp/archlinux.img2 : start=      206848, size=     1024000, type=83
```

Seems much more clear than:

```
- shell: >
    sfdisk -N2 < files/partitions
```

The second requires the `shell` module to perform input redirection,
and requires referencing another file just to find a small bit of
input.

We already have a `data` parameter in
`module_utils.basic.run_command`, so really this is just exposing it
via the `command` module.

title: A lifecycle for applying pre/post tasks within a role on dependent roles
body:
##### Issue Type:

Question / Feature Idea
##### Ansible Version:

```
ansible 1.9.4
  configured module search path = None
```
##### Ansible Configuration:

```
[defaults]
roles_path = roles:roles/external

[ssh_connection]
pipelining = True
ssh_args = -o ForwardAgent=yes
```
##### Environment:

```
Distributor ID: Ubuntu
Description:    Ubuntu 14.04.3 LTS
Release:        14.04
Codename:       trusty
```
##### Summary:

Is there any way to apply certain tasks within a role, right before the role dependencies run? I am familiar with `pre_tasks` and `post_tasks` as stated [here](https://github.com/ansible/ansible-examples/blob/master/language_features/roletest.yml) . See below why I'd like to avoid them.
##### Steps To Reproduce:

The simple playbook looks like:

```
- hosts: web
  tags:
    - web
  roles:
    - {role: nginx, sudo: yes, tags: [nginx]}
```

where role nginx depends on:

```
#meta/main.yml:
dependencies:
  - ansible-role-nginx # external role from Ansible Galaxy
```

Why such approach? 
I'd like to run certain tasks before role `ansible-role-nginx` is applied and these tasks are definitely role related (create some nginx directories). I think it sounds quite reasonable  to define a wrapper role and run whatever is needed there, before or after dependent roles. I'm trying to isolate my playbook and I'd like to avoid using  `pre_tasks` or `post_tasks`, because as the playbook grows, it's going to be a spaghetti code.
##### Expected Results:

A valid scenario to run certain tasks before/after dependent role is being applied.
##### Actual Results:

As I'm aware, the only way to run pre/post tasks for a role is to put them into playbook and use tags eventually to distinguish which tasks run for what role.

title: Run a handler immediately
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Core

##### SUMMARY
Sometimes it's useful to run a specific handler immediately after it was notified, without using `- meta: flush_handlers` to run all queued handlers, which isn't always desirable. A good example is when you are working with `systemd` unit files. After changing a unit file you always need to run `systemctl daemon-reload`, so you always have to use something like this:
```yml
- name: task which updates a unit file
  #...
  register: daemon_reload

- command: systemctl daemon-reload
  when: daemon_reload|changed
```
This is very inconvenient and repetitive, because you need to repeat the same exact register/command every time. It would be easier if you could define a handler, e.g:
```yml
- name: daemon-reload
  command: systemctl daemon-reload
```
and just call that handler to be executed immediately, e.g.:
```yml
- name: task which updates a unit file
  #...
  notify_now: daemon-reload
```
I used a `notify_now` keyword, but it could be named differently, or with an even different syntax, but just something which would run this handler immediately after the task which updates the unit file. The handler could even be defined in some role which could be put as a dependency to other roles.

title: GPG module and key lookup
body:
##### SUMMARY
Ansible could use a GPG module, for managing keyrings, importing and exporting keys, verifying and making signatures, and decrypting and encrypting data (both symmetric and non). Working with GPG keys, outside of the context of apt, is an extremely common sysadmin task which we can make it easier to automate. I think the addition of this module could be complemented by a "gpgkey" lookup which returns matching keys. I am on the fence about whether encryption, decryption, and sign+verify functions should be split into their own submodules.

I've seen no prior tickets or discussion of this, so I've taken on the task of developing it myself, and have already implemented a significant amount of the prospective functionality, and am creating this issue in order to track my progress. Please assign it to me. Hopefully this will be ready in time for 2.9. 

This is a project which could prove thorny/hairy to maintain going forward. For elucidation on why that is, I'd love to [quote](https://blog.patternsinthevoid.net/pretty-bad-protocolpeople.html) @isislovecruft: 

> "...largely due to GnuPG’s interface design which uses file descriptors, and only file descriptors, to speak a custom, potentially binary but often ascii, order dependent line protocol, whose line order, keywords, number of fields, and other details are subject to change between minor point versions of GnuPG. If that sounds like a special hell invented by some sort of unholy crossing between RMS and a rabid howler monkey: welcome to working with (or rather, more likely, around) the Terrible Idea Generator known as the GnuPG development team."

For now I've decided to rely on her version of python-gnupg which is now called pretty-bad-protoco] ([PyPi](https://pypi.org/project/pretty-bad-protocol/), [GitHub](https://github.com/isislovecruft/python-gnupg)); in my experience and the opinion of people I trust it's simply better, but that is a decision which the project/community will have to review when it's PR-time. 

I found two modules out there, but IMO neither of them are presently suitable for inclusion in core for various reasons. I want to note the prior effort by @tnt and @brandonkal anyway:
* https://github.com/tnt/ansible-gpg-import-module 
* https://github.com/brandonkal/ansible-gpg

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
- module: gpg
- lookup: gpgkey

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

My work-in-progress is located here: https://git.cointel.pro/FOSS/ansible-module-gpg

title: Feature Idea: Add support for multiple SSL in AWS ALB
body:
<!---
Verify first that your issue/request is not already reported on GitHub.
Also test if the latest release, and master branch are affected too.
-->

##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
elb_application_lb

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible --version
ansible 2.4.2.0
  config file = None
  configured module search path = [u'/root/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible
  executable location = /usr/local/bin/ansible
  python version = 2.7.13 (default, Nov 24 2017, 17:33:09) [GCC 6.3.0 20170516]
```

##### CONFIGURATION

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

##### SUMMARY
Since 10 october 2017, Application Load Balancer on AWS support multiple certificates SSL, so it would be awesome if Ansible can manage this.
Official announce : https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/new-application-load-balancer-sni/

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
I try to use the existing module "elb_application_lb" but when I set up multiple certificates in the Certificates parameters of the listener option, Ansible only set the last one.

```- elb_application_lb:
    name: myelb
    security_groups:
      - sg-12345678
    subnets:
      - subnet-012345678
    listeners:
      - Protocol: HTTPS
        Port: 443
        SslPolicy: ELBSecurityPolicy-2016-08
        Certificates: 
          - CertificateArn: arn:aws:iam::12345678987:server-certificate/test.domain.com
          - CertificateArn: arn:aws:iam::12345678987:server-certificate/foo.domain2.com
          - CertificateArn: arn:aws:iam::12345678987:server-certificate/bar.domain3.com
        DefaultActions:
          - Type: forward
            TargetGroupName: foo
    state: present
```
In this case, this playbook will only set the last certificate => arn:aws:iam::12345678987:server-certificate/bar.domain3.com


##### EXPECTED RESULTS

title: Proposal: Controlling when diff output should be produced
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
core

##### SUMMARY
Control when a diff is shown on a per-task level.

##### SOLUTIONS

Posibilities:
1. **Create a new special control `diff_when` using a condition**
   
   If there would be a need to control it independently from `changed_when`, an extra `diff_when` could be created that could have a different condition to control when a diff should be displayed.
2. **Create a new special control `show_diff` or `hide_diff` using a boolean only**
   
   Or we could decide that a special flag is sufficient that one has to explicitly flag as false or true in order to hide the diff output.

##### USE-CASE
One use-case is in fact what's already implemented in the puppet module. Since the diff may be leaking sensitive information (ansible/ansible-modules-extras#1187), they have added the opion `show_diff`, which at some point defaulted to `False`. If this was controllable from the task-level, individual modules wouldn't need their own switch.

##### NOTES
We may also want to look at providing flags to the diff routine. There is an open feature request (#14674) to ignore whitespace changes in the diff output. Since this depends on the file type/content this would make most sense to have on the task-level.

title: wishlist: be able to hide skipped roles when using conditional roles
body:
##### Issue Type: Feature Idea
##### Ansible Version: 1.7.1
##### Environment: Debian sid
##### Summary: hide print of tasks where the role is conditionally skipped
##### Steps To Reproduce: N/A
##### Expected Results:

All roles where the expression with 'when' is false should not be shown
at all, preferrably by a config var and perhaps a way to reverse
on the command line.
##### Actual Results: task are always shown even if the role is skipped

I think conditional roles is a great idea, it made my plays easier to write.

However, all those skipped tasks are still shown which makes it harder to see the more interesting
output from the tasks that really are used.

Would it be possible to somehow hide them, maybe a config option in ansible.cfg that could
be reversed by a command line option?
(Yes, I realize that it is also very useful to see what actually is there even if it is not used :-)

title: Pleaese reconsider deprecating hash_behaviour
body:
<!--- Verify first that your feature was not already discussed on GitHub -->
<!--- Complete *all* sections as described, this form is processed automatically -->

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Describe the new feature/improvement briefly below -->
Pleaese reconsider deprecating hash_behaviour

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Write the short name of the module, plugin, task or feature below, use your best guess if unsure -->
core

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
<!--- Describe how the feature would be used, why it is needed and what it would solve -->
There are use cases revolving around iterating over dicts and deduplication. Packing role parameters neatly into dicts, storing dozens of objects in dicts with ability to add or remove arbitrary objects on any group or host level.

So, for example, a role that manages user accounts, feeded by a dict of users
<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
# a dict containing dozens of users
accounts:
  bob:
    uid: 1000
    state: present
    # lots of attributes
  fred:
    uid: 1001
    # lots of attributes
  # lots of other users
```
Currently the role can enrich this dict with defaults and take in any adjustments on any level of inventory hierarchy as simple as:
```yaml
# group_vars/some_country_or_dc_or_isp_or_room_or_moon_phase_group
accounts:
  fred:
    state: absent
```
How would that be possible without hash merging? Provision a separate dict for every level of hierarchy and merge them all manually inside a role effectively duplicating inventory group structure?

Using prefixed variables for roles instead of dicts, or copypasting an entire dict to make a single change, or, again, provision a dict for every hierarchy level to be merged? This will add a lot of clutter.

Will `set_fact: { parameters: { subparameters: { option1: yes } } }` nuke an entire `parameters` dict?

<!--- HINT: You can also paste gist.github.com links for larger files -->

title: Support vault in password lookup plugin
body:
##### SUMMARY
It would be cool, if passwords generated with the password lookup plugin could be encrypted with Vault. This would allow for a streamlined distribution process that includes automatic password generation with record keeping (in git).

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
password lookup plugin
https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/devel/lib/ansible/plugins/lookup/password.py

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION

Vault support is, among others, included in the copy module and the [file lookup plugin](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/devel/lib/ansible/plugins/lookup/file.py). The latter using the [dataloader](https://github.com/ansible/ansiblehttps://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/devel/lib/ansible/parsing/dataloader.py)

My use case: Automatic generation of master users for dovecot proxy nodes. Each proxy needs a password, each backend server needs the hashes.
Right now the passwords are stored in clear text files and cannot get checked into git. Vault support in the plugin would make the password distribution process much smoother.

Thank you for your time and efforts.

title: Add plugin as parameter for mysql_user, to allow use of auth_socket plugin
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

`mysql_user`

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```shell
ansible 2.3.0.0
  config file =
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```

##### CONFIGURATION

Default configuration

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

N/A

##### SUMMARY

From the MySQL 5.7 what's new page:

> The server now requires account rows in the mysql.user table to have a nonempty plugin column value and disables accounts with an empty value.

On a fresh installation of MySQL 5.7 the `root` user for host `localhost` is now configured with a plugin value of `auth_socket`. This prevents users other then root to connect as root using the `mysql`client utility:

```shell
user@server$ mysql -u root -pP4ssw0rd
ERROR 1698 (28000): Access denied for user 'root'@'localhost'
```

While as root this works:

```shell
root@server# mysql
```

Currently, this cannot be done with Ansible.

References:

* https://dev.mysql.com/doc/refman/5.7/en/socket-pluggable-authentication.html
* https://www.slideshare.net/ottokekalainen/passwordless-login-with-unix-authsocket


##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

It would be nice to introduce a new parameter to this module which accepts all possible plugin values (defaulting to `mysql_native_password`) , e.g.:

```yaml
mysql_user: name=root password=abc123 plugin=mysql_native_password
mysql_user: name=root plugin=auth_socket
```

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

N/A

##### ACTUAL RESULTS

N/A

##### CHALLENGES

* @oscherler noted that the syntax varies between MySQL and MariaDB: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/pull/3587#issuecomment-227364601
* @wouteroostervld added that you have to change the plugin and password in one action, to avoid exposure: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/issues/3003#issuecomment-256328101

##### NOTES

* Issue migrated from https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/issues/3003
* Releated PR: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/pull/3587

I changed a few details:

* @verwilst mentioned that you can use either a password or auth_socket not both: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/issues/3003#issuecomment-199799891
* I updated the format to follow the latest one
* I updated the ansible version output, even if not very usefull in this case
* I also added some references and a challenges section

title: Support multiple vpc_id's when creating zone with route53_zone
body:
_From @oucil on December 7, 2016 21:1_

##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
route53_zone

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.3.0 (devel 14a2757116) last updated 2016/12/05 20:08:34 (GMT -400)
  lib/ansible/modules/core: (detached HEAD 6890003c4f) last updated 2016/12/05 20:08:36 (GMT -400)
  lib/ansible/modules/extras: (detached HEAD 20ea46642b) last updated 2016/12/05 20:08:37 (GMT -400)
  config file = ***
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```

##### CONFIGURATION
N/A

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY

We keep our ansible machine in a totally separate VPC ("mgmt") from the Production VPC ("prod") that it manages so that we can bring up or pull down development deployments ("dev") without touching the production deployment.  

Our playbook creates a new private zone in route53 for the new "dev" deployment, and includes the `vpc_id`, however, in order for ansible to have access to work with any of the instances we bring up in the new "dev" VPC, we need to ALSO attach the "mgmt" VPC to the Route53 zone so it can use the new hostnames.  We need to be able to specify more than one `vpc_id` for the zone to be attached to.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
```
- name: Create Route53 Private Zone
    route53_zone:
      region: "{{ aws.region }}"
      aws_access_key: "{{ aws.access_key }}"
      aws_secret_key: "{{ aws.secret_key }}"
      state: present
      zone: "{{ aws.route53_zone_internal }}"
      comment: "Dev internal DNS"
      # support list for input of vpc_id...
      vpc_id: ["{{ dev_vpc.vpc.id  }}","{{ aws.admin_vpc_id }}"]
      vpc_region: "{{ dev_vpc.vpc.region  }}"
```

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

Creation of a new Route 53 Zone, with 2 VPC ID's attached.


_Copied from original issue: ansible/ansible-modules-core#5860_

##### SUMMARY
A Java JAR file is a ZIP file with a manifest and can be extracted using unarchive.  A Java JAR file can also be executed in place.  It's pretty common to extract specific files from a Java JAR file (such as properties files) so that you can alter the execution environment.  In the case below, I simply want to extract the icon file to add a launcher for Burp Suite to the menu.
In any case, there are many other instances when it would be convenient to unarchive a limited set of files from an archive.  The "exclude" parameter provides a similar function, but wouldn't be convenient in this case as there are thousands of files and a complex directory hierarchy in this archive.  Since I'm not on 2.1 yet (Xubuntu), I don't have the exclude feature yet but used the command module to run unzip instead.  The only annoying aspect to this technique is that Ansible tells me I should be using the unarchive module.
Low priority but potentially very useful ... and thanks for hard work you all put in!

- name: extract the application's icon from the archive

           chdir={{ burp_installation_directory }}
           creates={{ burp_installation_directory }}/{{ burp_icon_path }}/{{ burp_icon_file }}

    - burp_suite

TASK [burp_suite : extract the application's icon from the archive] ************
changed: [localhost]
 [WARNING]: Consider using unarchive module rather than running unzip

Emilien

title: Integration with Mozilla SOPS for encrypted vars
body:
<!---
Verify first that your issue/request is not already reported on GitHub.
Also test if the latest release, and devel branch are affected too.
-->

##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
External variable repo source for decrypting vars via [Mozilla SOPS](https://github.com/mozilla/sops). SOPS can encrypt YAML and JSON files to multiple keys, which is appropriate for multiple admins sharing a single Ansible project repo, e.g. for managing a site config.

Although Ansible Vault has added support for [multiple vault_ids as of v2.4](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/2.4/vault.html#vault-ids-and-multiple-vault-passwords), SOPS supports multiple encryption backends such as GPG and AWS KMS. 

As for implementation, based on my understanding of the Ansible codebase, there are two outstanding candidates:

1. Custom vars_plugin via `lib/ansible/plugins/vars/`
2. Custom parser logic (as with ansible-vault) via `lib/ansible/parsing/`

The custom vars_plugin has the advantage of being applicable for drop-in to existing projects via a custom `vars_plugins` location specified in `ansible.cfg`, whereas parser changes would need to be added to the ansible core. Therefore I'll likely take that as first approach. 

See related issue on Ansible integration in the Mozilla SOPS repo for reference: https://github.com/mozilla/sops/issues/303

Any guidance on best practices for vars_plugin development would be welcome, as the [current dev docs on vars_plugins](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/devel/plugins/vars.html) are a bit scant. Stepping through `lib/ansible/plugins/vars/host_group_vars.py` should be a good enough primer to get started, so I'll begin there.

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
N/A; preferably 2.5 or later.

##### CONFIGURATION
N/A; feature request blocked pending prototype implementation.

title: The helm module should create a tunnel to tiller, the way helm cli does
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
helm

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.5.0
  config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = [u'/home/foo/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible
  executable location = /usr/bin/ansible
  python version = 2.7.14 (default, Sep 20 2017, 01:25:59) [GCC 7.2.0]
```

##### CONFIGURATION
DEFAULT_STDOUT_CALLBACK(/etc/ansible/ansible.cfg) = debug

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A (executed on Arch Linux)

##### SUMMARY
The command-line `helm` tool automatically creates a tunnel to the tiller server, so it doesn't need to be exposed on the internet.

The helm module does not.

That leaves the user with three bad choices:
- Expose tiller on the internet and take on all the risks that come with it
- Set up the tunnel manually, using shell+helm cli from ansible, use the module, then tear down the tunnel (undoing a lot of the benefits of using a native module along the way)
- Forget the module and just use shell+helm cli - no need to fiddle with tunnels (but lose the declarativeness and simplicity provided by a native module)

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
Use the module.

title: [virt] Overhaul and complete the virt_ modules
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

virt modules

##### SUMMARY

Add a new family oft libvirt modules.

The design and implementation of the libvirt modules

- [virt](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/virt_module.html)
- [virt_net](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/virt_net_module.html)
- [virt_pool](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/virt_pool_module.html)

is suboptimal. Some of the problems are

- Most operations are not idempotent
- No guarantee that operations have finished
- Important parameters are silently ignored or not handled as expected
- Many parameters have to be supplied as an XML document
- Important features, like a `virt_vol` module, are missing
- Code is not PEP8 compliant

##### RELATED ISSUES

- *virt module’s state change should be blocking* #19284
- *Module: virt_net is not idempotent (throws error when changed=False)* #26486
- *cloud/misc/virt.py does not support multiple args (e.g. state and autostart)* #26791
- *virt_net silently ignores autostart parameter if command is specified* #27064

##### CONCEPT

- Wait until operations have finished, using libvirt events
- Make some properties, currently only accessible via the XML API, available as module arguments
    - Makes is easy to change single properties (example: increase number of CPUs or add an HDD).
      Currently this would require to retrieve XML definitions and update XML elements. This in turn
      requires calls to command line tools for XML modification.
    - Allows to avoid using XML completely
- libvirt tasks describe target states of objects, not commands that are unconditionally executed
- Support live changes where possible

Planned modules

- libvirt_host
- libvirt_dom
- libvirt_net
- libvirt_pool
- libvirt_vol

title: Previous packages (.deb) should be kept on repository
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Bug / Workflow

##### COMPONENT NAME
Ansible's Ubuntu repository (version released)

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
All the version released for a specific distribution
```

##### SUMMARY
Ansible releases sometime bring new bugs that passes QA and are only found after release.

Like other project, it would make a lot more sense to keep all the releases for a distribution from inception until the distro support is dropped.
(See how Docker does it for example: https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/dists/xenial/pool/stable/amd64/ ). 
This allows to go back in time when things don't turn out as expected with newer versions.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
Upgrade to 2.2.2.0 and get bitten by https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/23016 without being able to rollback to 2.2.1.0 from the repository like any other package.

##### EXPECTED RESULTS
Being able to `apt-get install ansible=2.2.1.0-1ppa~xen` when 2.2.2.0 fails, apt-hold it and don't upgrade until the next fix comes out.
Also, allowing to test the new releases and being able to roll back in a coherent manner.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
The ansible repository:
http://ppa.launchpad.net/ansible/ansible/ubuntu/pool/main/a/ansible/
... only contains 2.2.2.0 which is an anti-pattern of Debian style repository, stopping people from testing the releases properly without reverting to pip or other workflow breaking solutions.

title: Check min_ansible_version for roles and don’t run the role if to old
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Ansible role min_ansible_version checking

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from “ansible --version” between quotes below -->
```
ansible 2.1.3.0
  config file = /home/user/.ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = ['/home/user/.ansible/ypid-ansible-common/ansible/library', '/usr/share/ansible/library']
```

##### CONFIGURATION

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
Debian stable

##### SUMMARY

Role authors can currently specify which Ansible version the role requires to run using `galaxy_info.min_ansible_version`.
It is pointless to attempt to run a role using a older Ansible version as the author probably had a reason to set the `min_ansible_version`.
However, Ansible does not even give a hint when running a role which requires a higher version.

The naming is kind of bad with `galaxy_info.min_ansible_version`, maybe when this feature idea gets implemented, the variable should be renamed.

Background:

As the recent release [fix two vulnerabilities](https://www.ansible.com/security), I would like to require at least `2.1.3`.
So also patch levels should be checked from `min_ansible_version`.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

Create a role with:

```
galaxy_info.min_ansible_version == '3.4.0'
```

in the `meta/main.yml` file. Create a playbook which runs the role. Run the Playbook.

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

Ansible should not attempt to run the role and show a error that the Ansible version requirement for some roles is not met.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS

Ansible runs the whole playbook including the role without a hint that the requirement of the role might be unmet.

When a task is marked as async, there is no way to loop until a condition is met.

title: feature request: it would be nice if a role could define a (implicit/explicit) tags
body:
This issue is similar to the issue #3283, which actually proposed exactly the same feature. I'm creating a new issue to bring attention back to this feature.

It seems useful to be able to run only single task from a playbook. It can be achieved by defining a tag in 'roles' section of the playbook. However this approach has drawback: in every playbook where we will use the role we will have to define the same tag (which may clutter the playbook and introduces duplication).

Implicit tag defined for each of the roles could be a option. Nice I think, since being able to run only selected role(s) seems to be useful. But it can also be considered as little mysterious. Maybe it would be better to introduce '--roles' parameter to ansible-playbook CLI instead.

The other option could be ability to define tags for a role in the role itself (instead of in the playbook). It could be done i.e. by extending role's meta/main.yml file by adding section 'tags' to it (next to 'dependencies') which would define role's tags. The tags defined in meta could sum up with the tags defined in playbook.
This way we could avoid duplication and avoid introducing the implicit tag.

title: Ability to temporarily mount a filesystem without altering /etc/fstab
body:
##### SUMMARY
Ability to perform temporary mounts without altering /etc/fstab.  Currently anything done with the mount module needs to manipulate /etc/fstab.

Additional parameter is needed to perform temporary mounts, such as with an ISO image that would be unmounted later in a play.  Currently this can't be done with the mount module, as altering the /etc/fstab is required.  Maybe call it "modify_fstab" and have it be a boolean.   Currently the only way to achieve this is via the command module using direct mount/unmount commands.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
mount, fstab

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
To mount an ISO image temporarily for pulling in data, but not make an entry in /etc/fstab.

```yaml
- name: Mount an ISO Temporarily
  mount:
    src: /path/to/my/iso.iso
    path: /path/to/mount/my/iso
    fstype: iso9660
    opts: ro
    modify_fstab: no
    state: mounted

- name: Unmount an ISO
  mount:
    path: /path/to/unmount/my/iso
    modify_fstab: no
    state: unmounted
```

`apt`

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
Ansible 2.5.0
```

##### CONFIGURATION

See: [`provision.yml`](https://github.com/kenorb/docker-dev-env-linux/blob/b5129c31230b59673e8c7b31327d8e434746843c/ansible/provision.yml).

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

Docker OS: Ubuntu
Host OS: macOS

##### SUMMARY

As per [Apt docs page](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/modules/apt_module.html), there is a param for `autoclean`, but not for `clean`. 

So I'd like to see the following configuration in place:

```
    - name: Apt - Removes all packages from the package cache
      apt: clean=yes
```

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

Currently, having the following lines (together or separately):

```
    - name: Apt - Remove packages from the cache
      apt: autoclean=yes
    - name: Apt - Remove dependencies that are no longer required
      apt: autoremove=yes
```

I would expect the old `.deb` files are removed, but they're not, since  `autoclean` is not the same as `clean`.

For the full config file, see: [`provision.yml`](https://github.com/kenorb/docker-dev-env-linux/blob/b5129c31230b59673e8c7b31327d8e434746843c/ansible/provision.yml).

Steps:

1. Given above configuration, I build my container. See Travis CI job: [#367880031](https://travis-ci.org/kenorb/docker-dev-env-linux/jobs/367880031) (line 668).
2. I see that my Apt `autoclean` and `autoremove` are called.
3. However `/var/cache/apt/archives/*.deb` files aren't removed.

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

After `apt: clean=yes` is implemented, I'd like to see all `/var/cache/apt/archives/*.deb` files being removed. Otherwise the size of my docker container is not as small as expected.


##### ACTUAL RESULTS

After executing `autoclean` and `autoremove`, I could still see a big `.deb` and other cache files such as:

```

  1194674  27696 -rw-r--r--   1 root     root     28357485 Apr 17 21:50 /var/cache/apt/srcpkgcache.bin
```

##### WORKAROUND

```
    - name: Apt - Removes all packages from the package cache
      command: apt-get clean
```

But then I've got the following warning:

> [WARNING]: Consider using the apt module rather than running apt-get.

title: no_log for specific variables/parameters
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Core

##### SUMMARY
`no_log` is great if you want to hide the output of some task, which is important especially if the task is working with secrets. The problem with `no_log` is that you cannot see what is going on with the task, which is especially important if it fails.

It would be really good if we could somehow mark specific variables not be logged, that way rest of the module would still be logged. To be honest I'm not sure if this is even possible, but my current idea is to have a filter `nolog` which does this. Here is an example:

```yml
---
- hosts: localhost
  vars:
    config:
      u1:
        user: user1
        pass: '{{ "F93EAQm" | nolog }}'
      u2:
        user: user2
        pass: '{{ "d4h6N31" | nolog }}'

  tasks:
    - name: some task
      ...
      with_dict: '{{ config }}''
```
In this example the value of any variable with the `nolog` filter set will not be displayed in the task output.

This is just an idea, which probably cannot work because of how filters work, but maybe there is a better solution to this problem. Any ideas?

Ansible currently lets you create IAM users and create managed IAM policies. However, it does not let you attach an existing managed IAM policy to an existing IAM user. - name: Attach managed policy to user

    policy_name: "{{policy_name}}"
    state: present
```

title: HTTP NTLM Auth support in 'uri' module
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
`uri`

##### SUMMARY
In looking at the [`uri` module](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/uri_module.html) it would be great if there were an option for NTLM authentication, such as there is on [`curl`](https://curl.haxx.se/docs/manpage.html#--ntlm).  I currently have to use a raw `command` module with Jinja2 templates to accomplish a callout, and then have to parse the response with another module entirely.  Being able to do this within the `uri` module would make my callout readable at least, as the `uri` module already has support for headers, POST requests, etc.

title: Pass ansible.cfg to Ansible via Python API
body:
##### Issue Type:

<!--- Please pick one and delete the rest: -->
- Feature Idea
##### Ansible Version:

```
ansible 2.0.1.0
  config file = /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```
##### Ansible Configuration:

<!---
Please mention any settings you have changed/added/removed in ansible.cfg
(or using the ANSIBLE_* environment variables).
-->
##### Environment:

N/A
##### Summary:

Currently `ansible.cfg` can be passed into ansible like:

```
* ANSIBLE_CONFIG (an environment variable)
* ansible.cfg (in the current directory)
* .ansible.cfg (in the home directory)
* /etc/ansible/ansible.cfg
```

Which is at [`/lib/ansible/constants.py#L85-L108`](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/v2.0.1.0-1/lib/ansible/constants.py#L85-L108).

However I couldn't find a way to pass ansible.cfg to ansible programmatically or via its Python API.

Possible Workarounds:
- **Set `ANSIBLE_CONFIG` ENV var**: works but limits the program to run one Playbook at the time on the current process, doesn't work when I have a alive process such as web app on REST API that does the playbook runnings and reporting for me.
- **Keep `ansible.cfg` in the current/any directory**: Not sure how tell Ansible via python API about the Playbook directory and let it know about the `ansible.cfg` in that dir, since [`load_config_file`](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/v2.0.1.0-1/lib/ansible/constants.py#L85) is using `os.getcwd()` to get it.

This is a feature request that would help working with Ansible via Python API much easier, especially now when we run many playbooks via our web app. 

I'd love to send a pull-request for this feature, but I don't know exactly what would be a good implementation without making API bleeding.

Possible Implementations:
- Explicitly pass `ansible.cfg` file name/path to [`load_config_file`](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/v2.0.1.0-1/lib/ansible/constants.py#L85).

title: Feature Request: Use git-lfs with ansible modules copy and unarchive, and possibly other modules.
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
core

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
2.2

##### CONFIGURATION


##### OS / ENVIRONMENT


##### SUMMARY
git-lfs (Large File Storage) is catching on and is available in both GitHub and GitLab as of late 2015. It would be nice to include large files in a role/files directory and track them with git lfs. In git-lfs, these are text reference pointers to the file that can be fetched from the git server. 

The feature request: File modules such as 'copy' and 'unarchive' should check for the text reference pointer and if found, automatically transfer the file from the git server (git lfs) directly to the destination hosts.

While I could clone the full git repo and fetch all the files, this would cause two full transfers of potentially GB's of data, vs just one as described above. 

Perhaps another feature request should be add git-lfs support to the 'git' module as well?

title: Per var merging of lists (and hashes?)
body:
##### SUMMARY
There is value in being able to merge/overload lists/dicts **on a case-by-case basis** at *group_vars* level, i.e. having an empty list at *all* group that can be populated depending on the groups a host belongs to.

this issue is a duplicate of #18257 (which, IMHO, was closed without offering a suitable solution).

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
group_vars / host_vars

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
Use case:
Say, *aserver* belongs to *[mysql]*, *[common]* and *[middleware]* *host groups*, and on each group_vars file you find something like this:

* *group_vars/all*:
  ```yaml
  redhat_repos: []
  ```
* *groups_vars/common*:
  ```yaml
  redhat_repos: "{{ redhat_repos | union(['rhel', 'rhel-optional']) }}"
  ```
* *group_vars/mysql*:
  ```yaml
  redhat_repos: "{{ redhat_repos | union(['custom-mysql-repo']) }}"
  ```
* *group_vars/middleware*:
  ```yaml
  redhat_repos: "{{ redhat_repos | union(['custom-tomcat-repo']) }}"
  ```

...so, in the end, *redhat_repos* becomes:
- rhel
- rhel-optional
- custom-mysql-repo
- custom-tomcat-repo

Note the above does **not** work as per Ansible 2.9.2 up to 2.12.14: it fails with an unhandled exception because of *'recursive loop detected in template string'*.

Same concept is of relevance for hashes and combine filter too.

**NOTE:** I'm aware of *DEFAULT_HASH_BEHAVIOUR* configuration option, but that's global, non-portable, warned as deprecated by development team and leads to unexpected side-effects, so I don't think it's the solution I'm looking for.

title: Do not produce a warning when setting errors='ignore' in lookup()
body:
##### SUMMARY
The lookup plugin has an option `errors` which can be either `strict`, `warn` or `ignore`  
But `warn` produces 2 warnings per error and `ignore` produces one as demonstrated in the documentation : https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/plugins/lookup.html#id5

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Lookup plugins

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
Have one meaningful warning per lookup use when using `warn` and none when using `ignore`

`warn`
```
- name: file doesnt exist, but i dont care .. file plugin itself warns anyways ...
  debug: msg="{{ lookup('file', '/idontexist', errors='ignore') }}"

[WARNING]: Unable to find '/idontexist' in expected paths (use -vvvvv to see paths)

ok: [localhost] => {
    "msg": ""
}
```

`ignore` : 
```
- name: file doesnt exist, but i dont care .. file plugin itself warns anyways ...
  debug: msg="{{ lookup('file', '/idontexist', errors='ignore') }}"

ok: [localhost] => {
    "msg": ""
}
```

##### CONTEXT
I want to copy the content of files whose name match one of the groups of the current host, so I loop over the host's groups but I get a warning every time the file doesn't exist
```yaml
- blockinfile:
      path: /my/file
      marker: "# {mark} {{ group }}"
      #block: "{{ lookup('template', group~'.j2', errors='ignore') }}"
      block: "{{ lookup('template', group~'.j2', errors='warn') }}"
    loop: "{{ group_names }}"
    loop_control:
      loop_var: group
```

I can't split it in a `local_action: stat` loop'd + `blockinfile` over the register because the playbook repository path is unpredictable in my context...

title: docker_container should support --mount option
body:
##### SUMMARY

docker_container should support --mount option, since that is the recommended way to bind volumes to a container according to https://docs.docker.com/storage/bind-mounts/

title: ini_file: Support multiple option=value pairs
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME

ini_file

##### ANSIBLE VERSION

2.4

##### SUMMARY

The ini_file module is nice for updating single entries but very slow when inserting a whole section. This is especially important when the system may reboot for no good reason and the program analysing the .ini file dies or severely misbehaves when a section is incomplete.

Thus I propose that, when there is no `option` parameter, the `value` may be a dict of option/value pairs which are all set at once, in a single Ansible transaction.

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

```
ini_file:
    path: /tmp/test.ini
    section: plugh
    value:
        foo: bar
        baz: quux
```
results in
```
$ cat /tmp/test/ini
[plugh]
foo = bar
baz = quux
```

title: Check if service is installed just with conditionals
body:
From @guenhter on 2016-10-21T08:54:36Z
##### ISSUE TYPE

<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
- Feature Idea
##### COMPONENT NAME

service
##### ANSIBLE VERSION

<!--- Paste verbatim output from “ansible --version” between quotes below -->

```
ansible 2.2.0.0 (detached HEAD bce9bfce51) last updated 2016/10/20 17:00:41 (GMT +000)
```
##### SUMMARY

When I want to ensure, that a certain program is NOT running, I use the `service` module and set the parameter `state: stopped`. This works perfectly fine, when the service is really installed. 
But in many cases, the service isn't installed and than this module just fails. 
This is really annoying, because a task must be added before the service task, which checks somehow if the service is even installed. 
It would be very helpful, to somehow extend the service-module to stop a service only if it is available. 

In my point of view the most generic solution would be to add some Utility usable in the when block:

```
- service
    name: topbeat
    state: stopped
  when: services.state.topbeat is present
```

Copied from original issue: ansible/ansible-modules-core#5346

title: core-module 'user' with option 'expires' - cannot "unexpire" accounts with "-1"
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ansible-modules-core/system/user.py

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.1.1.0
```

##### CONFIGURATION

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
Ubuntu 16.04

##### SUMMARY
The module user is supposed to be based on system commands "useradd, userdel and usermod". It has no account locking function because people discussed that an expire date option would be better. So to disable a bunch of users I can use 'expires' with time in epoch format, but ...

.. the problem is, **that you cannot reset the expire date (disable expiry)** as you could with the system commands. Usually, you can use the expiredate option of e.g. adduser and set it to "-1" or nothing (emtpy string):

The man pages for adduser/useradd say: "By default, **the password expiry value set to -1** means never expire." Furthermore, adduser allows to set an empty string: "If not specified, useradd will use the default expiry date specified by the EXPIRE variable in /etc/default/useradd, **or an empty string (no expiry)** by default."

Since this module uses system commands, I would expect it to offer similiar functionality but it does not:
- if you set expires=-1 it does not change anything at all, account stays expired. YOu can't use an empty string either: -> "msg": "argument expires is of type <type 'str'> and we were unable to convert to float"

In terms of shell you can easily unset expire date with: usermod USER -e -1

I would suggest that it should be able to use the module "user" as you would use adduser/moduser option --expiredate. Until now, you would have to use the command/shell/raw module. It would make sense to me to enable this for the user module.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE

First, expire a user:

```yaml
---
- name: test expire
  hosts: all
  become: yes
  vars_files:
    - /etc/ansible/secrets.yml
  tasks:
    - name: Disable user accounts with expires
      user: name={{ item.name }} expires=1

      with_items:
        - { name: 'USER' }
```
Now, "unexpire" the user with expires=-1  or expires="" or even expires=

```yaml
---
- name: test expire
  hosts: all
  become: yes
  vars_files:
    - /etc/ansible/secrets.yml
  tasks:
    - name: Disable user accounts with expires
      user: name={{ item.name }} expires=-1

      with_items:
        - { name: 'USER' }
```
##### EXPECTED RESULTS

It should re-enable the user account, unsetting any expire date as it would with usermod/moduser

##### ACTUAL RESULTS

With "-1" ansible tells you "changed: [HOST] => (item={u'name': u'USER'} but it has changed nothing:
Your account has expired; please contact your system administrator

If you use an empty string for expire, it fails:
failed: [HOST] (item={u'name': u'USER'}) => {"failed": true, "item": {"name": "USER"}, "msg": "argument expires is of type <type 'str'> and we were unable to convert to float"

title: Feature: Extended Vault support: copying files, lookup plugin
body:
When there's a need to keep some sensitive data close to a playbook, Vault is great for ciphering variables. However, what about encrypting files that are transferred to the target host?

Suggesting the following:
- Decrypt Vault-protected files on-the-fly with the `copy` module
- Implement a lookup plugin to read data from Vault-protected files, like `lookup('vault', 'path/to/file')`

title: Ad-Hoc gather facts before executing task
body:
##### SUMMARY
Add a parameter to gather facts before executing the Ad-Hoc task.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Ad-Hoc

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
I want to have an adhoc task gather facts before it runs so that I can test my jinja2 expressions using `ansible -m debug -a msg="{{ hostvars['localhost']['ansible_facts'][...] }}"`-style commands.

Currently one can either print a jinja2 expression (without facts being retrieved) or gather facts using `ansible -m setup`.

Possible implementations:
* add a new parameter `-g, --gather-facts`
* another implementation could be to allow an Ad-Hoc task to execute more than one module sequentially.
* If it is already possible, please add documentation here https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/user_guide/intro_adhoc.html#gathering-facts

title: Provide network interface facts as a dictionary
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
`setup` module

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.5.0
  config file = None
  configured module search path = [u'/home/nst/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/ansible
  executable location = /usr/local/bin/ansible
  python version = 2.7.6 (default, Oct 26 2016, 20:30:19) [GCC 4.8.4]
```

##### SUMMARY
Currently, facts about network interfaces are only present in the root of the fact hierarchy (with names like `ansible_eth0`), while facts about disk devices are grouped in a dictionary `ansible_devices`.

**I propose making the network interface facts available as a dictionary** (similar to what's done for disk devices) in the existing `ansible_interfaces` (which is currently only a list of names).

The dictionary approach is preferable, as it:
* reduces clutter in the hierarchy root,
* avoids name collisions (imagine someone named their network interface "dns", for example), and
* makes it much easier and "cleaner" to access those facts dynamically (see example playbook).

This change can be implemented in a backwards-compatible way due to the sematics of Python iterators:  
`for x in ["lo", "eth0"]` iterates over list *items*, i.e. `lo` and `eth0`  
`for x in {"lo": {…}, "eth0": {…}}` iterates over dictionary *keys*, i.e. `lo` and `eth0`

##### CURRENT FACT CONTENT
```javascript
        "ansible_interfaces": [
            "lo",
            "eth0"
        ],
        "ansible_lo": {
            "active": true, 
            "device": "lo", 
            "features": { … },
            "hw_timestamp_filters": [], 
            "ipv4": { … },
            …
        },
        …
```

##### SUGGESTED FACT CONTENT
```javascript
        "ansible_interfaces": {
            "lo": {
                "active": true, 
                "device": "lo", 
                "features": { … },
                "hw_timestamp_filters": [], 
                "ipv4": { … },
                …
            },
            "eth0": {
                "active": true, 
                "device": "eth0", 
                "features": { … },
                "hw_timestamp_filters": [], 
                "ipv4": { … },
                …
            },
        ], 
```

… and for backwards-compatibility, probably also

```javascript
        "ansible_lo": {
            "active": true, 
            "device": "lo", 
            "features": { … },
            "hw_timestamp_filters": [], 
            "ipv4": { … },
            …
        },
```

##### EXAMPLE PLAYBOOK

Let's say we want to determine the speed of the primary network device and use it in a template (I'm substituting `msg` of the `debug` module for an actual template file).

Currently, that requires some trickery like this:

```yaml
- hosts: all
  tasks:
      - debug: 'msg="{{vars[\"ansible_\

title: inventory_hostnames lookup doesn't support wildcards in patterns
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
- Bug Report
##### COMPONENT NAME

inventory_hostnames lookup plugin
##### ANSIBLE VERSION

```
ansible 2.1.1.0
```
##### CONFIGURATION

**ansible.cfg**

``` ini
[defaults]
host_key_checking = False
#ask_pass = True
remote_user = root
retry_files_enabled = False
```
##### OS / ENVIRONMENT

CentOS 7
##### SUMMARY

When using wildcards in patterns with the `with_inventory_hostnames` lookup plugin, no results are returned. Using explicit patterns (`all:!web`) works.

There are scenarios where we want to use `with_inventory_hostnames` to iterate over a specific subset of a larger inventory, without always having to create a group for what may be a one-off command.
##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
1. Create inventory and playbook files as shown below

**inv**

``` ini
[web]
dzcustomer1web1
dzcustomer1svc2
dzcustomer2web1
dzcustomer3web1
[app]
opcustomer1app1
opcustomer2app2
opcustomer3app3
```

**test.yml**

``` yml

---
- hosts: localhost
  tasks:
    - debug:
        var: item
      with_inventory_hostnames: *customer1*
```
1. Run playbook
##### EXPECTED RESULTS

Expected that the wildcard pattern would be supported the same way it is in the `-l` flag.

``` shell
$ ansible-playbook -i inv test.yml

PLAY [localhost] ***************************************************************

TASK [setup] *******************************************************************
ok: [localhost]

TASK [debug] *******************************************************************
ok: [localhost] => (item=dzcustomer1web1) => {
    "item": "dzcustomer1web1"
}
ok: [localhost] => (item=dzcustomer1svc2) => {
    "item": "dzcustomer1svc2"
}
ok: [localhost] => (item=opcustomer1app1) => {
    "item": "opcustomer1app1"
}

PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************
localhost                  : ok=1    changed=0    unreachable=0    failed=0
```
##### ACTUAL RESULTS

``` shell
$ ansible-playbook -i inv test.yml

PLAY [localhost] ***************************************************************

TASK [setup] *******************************************************************
ok: [localhost]

TASK [debug] *******************************************************************

PLAY RECAP *********************************************************************
localhost                  : ok=1    changed=0    unreachable=0    failed=0
```

We've tested this with the filter configured multiple ways, all of which fail. Only explicit mentions appear to work as outlined in the [patterns](http://docs.ansible.com/ansible/intro_patterns.html) documentation. These patterns do work when used with `-l`, though.

```
*customer1*
"*customer1*"
*web1
dz*
```

title: add vault/main.yml to roles and include by default
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
vault

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
N/A

##### SUMMARY
Hi guys,

We are using Ansible Vault in order to secure our sensible data. In order to keep our roles self-contained, we use the following role structure:

``` yaml
### Extract from myrole/tasks/main.yaml

---
# Include sensible data
- name: Include encrypted vars
  include_vars: "{{ item }}"
  with_first_found:
    - "{{ aws_account }}/vault.yaml"
    - "vault.yaml"

# Include role's tasks
- include: configure.yaml
```

Given that the `include_vars` statement, we loose the ability to do `--start-at-task "One specific task from configure.yaml"` because we might need a variable defined in the `vault.yaml` file.

It could be great if Ansible by default load a "default" vault file the same way it loads `defaults/main.yaml` or `vars/main.yaml` (Why not a `vault/main.yaml` ?).

Most of the time our use case is to encrypt AWS credentials that are used in our roles (Either to configure a software or to simply download the software from an S3 bucket).

We don't want to have the playbook dealing with vault file includes as it could become really messy:

Example:

``` yaml

---
  host: all
  vars_files:
    ### I do not want to check each role I am using in my playbook to know which one is using a vault file that I need to include!
    - roles/???/vars/vault.yaml
    - roles/???/vars/vault.yaml
    - roles/???/vars/vault.yaml
    ...
  roles: 
    - common
    - aws-cli
    - user
    - java
    - logstash-client
    - ganglia
    - nagios
```

Feel free to comment if you know an other way to solve this problem.

Thanks a lot.

title: Add support for yum options related to security
body:
#### ISSUE TYPE
Feature Idea

#### COMPONENT NAME
yum module

#### SUMMARY
yum these days comes with the ability to perform the following security related actions:

```
yum update --security # updates only the packages that need security related updates
yum update --cve CVE-2015-4000 # updates all packages with fixes for the given CVE
yum update --advisory=ALAS-2015-551 # updates packages in the scope of the given security advisory
```

As a person who administers fleets of servers, I often want to perform fixes that are security driven, and the above operations come in handy.

Ansible, however, has no support for these as of now.

Here's a page explaining these features: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/10021

title: shell module: option for interactive/login shell
body:
I was trying to execute a `shell` command using a specific user. I was having problem to execute that shell command with the same invocation method as when the user normally logs in (`login shell`).

The way to solve it was to specify a `-i` at the end of the `sudo_user` parameter like this:

``` yaml

---
- name: rbenv | check ruby installed
  shell: rbenv versions | grep {{ ruby_version }}
  sudo_user: rails -i
  register: ruby_installed
  ignore_errors: yes
```

It'd be nice if the `shell` module had an option to specify the shell invocation method.

title: Bump ansible-core controller minimum to Python 3.8
body:
##### SUMMARY
Testing and supporting older Pythons for the Ansible controller has an ever-increasing cost, especially as new Python releases are now coming yearly. With the sunset of Python 2.7, and in light of our current feature roadmap, we've set Python 3.8 as the minimum supported controller Python version for Ansible 2.11 and the release to follow it. To be clear: **this does not affect the supported minimum Python version on remote targets** (which will remain at **Python 2.6** for the foreseeable future).

The Python 3.8 requirement for Ansible 2.11 will be "soft", in that breaking code changes that require Python 3.8 will not be made, but testing of older controller Python versions will stop during the 2.11 development cycle, and official Ansible packages will only be released for Python >= 3.8 (possibly including PyPI `python_requires` metadata, still TBD). The Ansible 2.12 controller will introduce code changes that will actively break compatibility with older Python versions.

The change will occur in a few phases:
* ansible-core 2.11 (currently `devel` branch) warning on Python < 3.8 (complete: https://github.com/ansible/ansible/pull/72467)
* Cessation of controller testing on Python < 3.8 (TBD, depends on CI changes to support split controller/remote testing)
* PyPI `python_requires` metadata update (TBD, if done, this would require passing `--ignore-requires-python` to `pip` to install on unsupported Python versions)
* ansible-core 2.11 beta/general release
* ansible-core 2.12 `devel` code changes that use Python 3.8 features

Additionally, there are related projects during 2.11 to maintain compatibility with controller-run modules like `yum`, `dnf` and selinux-aware modules that rely on OS-provided libraries like `libselinux-python`. The result will be that these modules will transparently run under the system Python version.

- Feature Idea

title: Add z/OS prompt to SU_PROMPT_LOCALIZATIONS
body:
### Summary

Unlike most operating systems that have keyword `password` at the prompt for privilege escalation, the Unix System Services (USS) on the z/OS operating system prompt is actually `FSUM5019 Enter the password for IBMUSER: `. 

I would like to add the keyword [FSUM5019](https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/zos/2.4.0?topic=fsum-fsum5019) to the [SU_PROMPT_LOCALIZATIONS](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/blob/c404a9003fbfc56785d32e3f6e6ab005d0467927/lib/ansible/plugins/become/su.py#L99) list. Doing so will save many developers time diagnosing and trouble shooting why they are getting a timeout when Ansible is not designed to expect such a prompt and thus needing to be statically added to ansbibe.cfg with `localized_prompts`. 

I have added this and tested this very simple change in both Ansible 2.10 and 2.11 with success in conjunction with the bug I have reported in [git issue 75076](https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/75076).

The change is simple, append 'FSUM5019' to:
```
    SU_PROMPT_LOCALIZATIONS = [
        'Password',
        '암호',
        'パスワード',
        'Adgangskode',
        'Contraseña',
        'Contrasenya',
        'Hasło',
        'Heslo',
        'Jelszó',
        'Lösenord',
        'Mật khẩu',
        'Mot de passe',
        'Parola',
        'Parool',
        'Pasahitza',
        'Passord',
        'Passwort',
        'Salasana',
        'Sandi',
        'Senha',
        'Wachtwoord',
        'ססמה',
        'Лозинка',
        'Парола',
        'Пароль',
        'गुप्तशब्द',
        'शब्दकूट',
        'సంకేతపదము',
        'හස්පදය',
        '密码',
        '密碼',
        '口令',
        'FSUM5019',
    ]
```



### Issue Type

Feature Idea

### Component Name

ansible.builtin.su

### Additional Information

<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml (paste below)

    - name: Who are we on the z/OS target with privilege escalation
      command: whoami
      become: yes
      become_method: su
      become_user: omvsadm
      register: result

    - name: Who did we run as
      debug:
        var: result
```


### Code of Conduct

- [X] I agree to follow the Ansible Code of Conduct

title: Option to promote warnings to errors
body:
##### SUMMARY
When using CI/CD (and probably other use cases) we want to make sure all warnings are dealt with and resolved. Parsing the output is error prone, so ideally ansible would return a non-zero return code when a warning is thrown.

The suggestion is that we add a new parameter or configuration directive that would activate this behavious

It could be achieved in two ways:

1) turn warnings into fatal errors - which would stop the run at that point and return a non-zero code

2) flag when warnings occurred and return a non-zero code but without stopping the current run

My suggestion is for option 2 as that allows the CI to complete, gather all other possible failures, but prevent the pipeline from becoming "green".

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
playbook run

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
- #42479 -- but this is addresses only one type of warning
- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50031257/ansible-promoting-warnings-to-errors/57121991
