title: Snapcraft: Add 'snap connect'
body:
##### SUMMARY
Because of the containerization of snaps, they often require explicit connection to other services, using the command `snap connect [app]:[plug]`. Right now, this can only be done with the `shell` cmd.

##### ISSUE TYPE
I would like to propose a snap module addition to support the `snap connect` commands. For the sake of full feature set, it would also be preferable to return currently connected snaps in a return value.

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Write the short name of the module, plugin, task or feature below, use your best guess if unsure -->

snap.py

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
<!--- Describe how the feature would be used, why it is needed and what it would solve -->

Currently, I run commands like this:

```yaml
    - name: Snap Packages - Standard Installs
      become: yes
      become_method: sudo
      snap:
        name:
          - nextcloud-client
          - remmina

    - name: Snap Packages - Setup Plugs
      become: yes
      become_method: sudo
      changed_when: false
      shell: 'snap connect {{ item.app }}:{{ item.plug }}'
      with_items:
        - plug: password-manager-service
          app: nextcloud-client
        - plug: avahi-observe
          app: remmina
```

Ideally, it should be configurable with something similar to:
<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml

    - name: Snap Packages - Standard Installs
      become: yes
      become_method: sudo
      snap:
        name:
          - nextcloud-client
          - remmina
        connect:
          - plug: password-manager-service
            app: nextcloud-client
          - plug: avahi-observe
            app: remmina

```

title: Re-run failed tasks after rescue
body:
##### SUMMARY
If a rescue is triggered (and executes successfully), the block/task it was rescuing should be able to be re-executed.

##### ISSUE TYPE
- Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Ansible Block/Rescue

##### ADDITIONAL INFORMATION
It would be nice to have an option to retry the block after rescue so we avoid coping the block tasks into the rescue section (DRY).

Note that this is distinct from e.g. #8896. Using `retries` and `until` doesn't allow me to run a rescue task in between to clean up the mess.

```yaml
- name: ensure symlink exists
  block:
    file:
      path: /my/symlink
      src: /my/actual/dir
      state: link
      force: yes
  rescue:
  - name: fix it up
    file:
      path: /my/symlink
      state: absent
    redo_task: yes
```

I can't make `file` replace a (non-empty) directory even with `force` so I need the extra step. But if it's already a symlink I don't want to remove it and recreate it.

This seems like it would be a cleaner approach than:

- Having a separate `stat` task or similar just to check for this condition
- Always running the repair task in advance, even if it's not needed

Even beyond this specific use case it seems more natural to be able to have your playbook act on expected/normal conditions and use `rescue` to clean up corner cases (without duplicating block tasks in `rescue`). Otherwise you have to have what amounts to a pre-task to check for corner cases in advance and repair them, which seems inefficient to me.

In my example `redo_task` could be named/placed however is appropriate for `block`/`rescue` syntax but you get the idea.

title: Add working directory variable by default
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
manager

##### SUMMARY
Request a built-in variable `awd` for ansible working directory. Expected result of the implementation:
```bash
ansible-playbook --extra-vars "awd=$(pwd)"
```

##### Why?
There are several use-cases where a role might need a reference to the ansible working directory or root directory structure of the [scm] repository, and in many cases, stacks of relative `../..` references can be cumbersome to manage or difficult to read. References to configuration files may not always be encapsulated in the role or playbook directory structure, depending on usage.

```yaml
- include_vars:
    file: '{{ awd }}/config/users.yml'
```

##### Why not use `playbook_dir` variable?
It's not consistent for determining the base path. There is a use-case for nesting playbooks into directory structures, based on their intent. Determining relative paths from a nested playbook directory structure can be cumbersome. Additionally, this structure can vary based on usage.

##### Why not just set with `pwd` at runtime OR as a variable?
This is what I currently do. The idea is to create a variable consistent with the working directory. One that does not change based on implementation or usage.

managing, or say "N/A" for anything that is not platform-specific.
Also mention the specific version of what you are trying to control,
e.g. if this is a network bug the version of firmware on the network device.
-->
N/A

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->
Not sure if it's been discussed before, but I feel like having [ipset](http://www.firewalld.org/documentation/man-pages/firewalld.ipset.html) support in the firewalld module would be pretty cool. I know that for *my* use-case it would definitely reduce my need to use firewalld with a `with_items` clause.

Unfortunately, I haven't spent much time looking at the structure of the firewalld module to be able to gauge the amount of work necessary to get it implemented. Just an idea at this point.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, show exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case.
For new features, show how the feature would be used.
-->

Definitely open to change the idea of how this would look, more or less just a first pass at thinking about it:
<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
- firewalld:
    service: https
    permanent: true
    state: enabled
    ipset:
      - 192.168.0.1
      - 192.168.0.13
      - 192.168.0.18
      - 192.168.0.20
```

Though since you can create named ipsets, maybe it would make more sense to have a separate ansible module to create and manage ipsets, then maybe we could add support to this module to interface with those created ipsets? That could look like this:

```yaml
- firewalld_ipset:
    name: nameserver_clients
    description: an ipset of my name servers
    sources:
      - 192.168.0.1
      - 192.168.0.13
      - 192.168.0.18
      - 192.168.0.20

- firewalld:
    service: dns
    permanent: true
    state: enabled
    ipset: nameserver_clients
```

Just some thoughts to kick around, very open to changing or adapting them. Also happy to draft a PR for it

title: grafana annotation callback

##### ISSUE TYPE

 - Feature Idea

plugins/callback/grafana.py

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.4.2.0
  config file = None
  configured module search path = [u'/Users/remirey/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /Users/remirey/.venv/reminder/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ansible

  python version = 2.7.13 (default, Apr  4 2017, 08:47:57) [GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 8.1.0 (clang-802.0.38)]
```

##### CONFIGURATION
N/A

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY

Grafana v4.6+ comes with a native annotation store and the ability to add annotation events directly from the graph panel or via the HTTP API.

An Ansible callback could publish global annotations to Grafana through the HTTP API when the callback is whitelisted.

The annotations are extremely useful to notify of an intervention through ansible playbook.
With the callback, each playbook execution would automatically imply a new annotation to be published on the grafana dashboard, allowing to see immediately on the monitoring side that something was done through playbook.

It could accelerate investigations and allow to associate metrics variations to a playbook execution.

title: Each ansible module should have some configurable "max_time" threshold and watchdog that would fail the task if it takes longer than defined time
body:
<!---
Verify first that your issue/request is not already reported on GitHub.
Also test if the latest release, and master branch are affected too.
-->

##### ISSUE TYPE
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
Ansible

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
Future version I guess...

##### CONFIGURATION
It could look like this in config file, but should be configurable per task, playbook etc. Tasks that run long time (like package) should have some kind of exception. Also this should have some sane defaults per task, for example modules like setup, lineinfile, etc. should never take longer than 2 minutes.
```
# Maximum threshold in seconds that any single task is allowed to run, otherwise it's considered failed
task_timeout = 120
```

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
All of them

##### SUMMARY
Right now Ansible randomly hangs https://github.com/ansible/ansible/issues/30411

That is often caused by trivial issues, like NFS mounts being broken on target servers. On these servers even commands like "df" hang forever. If you execute playbook the "setup" module would hang for years on such servers right now.

I also noticed a trivial "service" module to lock up for 5 hours for unknown reasons, I had to kill ansible by hand to stop it.

There should be some timeout parameter and watchdog that would deliberately abort the task if it exceeds the allowed time.

title: ec2_metric_alarm module missing option treat_missing_data
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
ec2_metric_alarm

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
2.3.2.0
```

##### CONFIGURATION
N/A

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY
Amazon CloudWatch has added a setting to define how an alarm handles a missing data point ([announcement](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2017/03/cloudwatch-alarms-releases-two-new-alarm-configuration-settings/), [description](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/AlarmThatSendsEmail.html#alarms-and-missing-data)) using:
* "Treat missing data as:" in the [UI](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/ConsoleAlarms.html)
* `--treat-missing-data` option in the [AWS CLI](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/cloudwatch/put-metric-alarm.html#synopsis)
* `TreatMissingData` request parameter in the [API](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/APIReference/API_PutMetricAlarm.html)


Ansible needs a `treat_missing_data` option in its ec2_metric_alarm module with possible values `breaching` (bad), `notBreaching` (good), `ignore`, and `missing`.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
- ec2_metric_alarm:
    ...
    treat_missing_data: notBreaching
    ...
```

Also, Terraform has the [treat_missing_data](https://www.terraform.io/docs/providers/aws/r/cloudwatch_metric_alarm.html#treat_missing_data) argument for its aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm provider resource.

<!--- What did you expect to happen when running the steps above? -->

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
<!--- What actually happened? If possible run with extra verbosity (-vvvv) -->

<!--- Paste verbatim command output between quotes below -->
```

```

From the original issue:

version
`ansible v2, devel`

issue
when changing multiple yum repos (e.g. in a common/base role), it can be necessary to clean the cache so yum will refresh and any future actions will get the updated meta.

current
it's necessary to either run a shell/command,

- command: yum clean all

which currently gives a warning [WARNING]: Consider using yum module rather than running yum

Or put update_cache: yes in at least one yum (present, installed, latest) task after the repo changes. If there isn't such a yum task after repo changes, then we don't get the updates. Which means we'd need to put update_cache: yes elsewhere we use yum - which is likely overkill.

desired

    the ability to use update_cache as a standalone action
    (optional) a param to `clean XYZ`.

### LINK: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/issues/2667

title: get_url should support acquiring checksum from remote source
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
get_url

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
```
ansible 2.4.0 (devel b629e36d3b) last updated 2017/07/20 15:14:54 (GMT +1100)
  config file = None
  configured module search path = [u'/home/will/.ansible/plugins/modules', u'/usr/share/ansible/plugins/modules']
  ansible python module location = /home/will/src/ansible-work/ansible/lib/ansible
  executable location = /usr/local/bin/ansible
  python version = 2.7.13 (default, Jan 19 2017, 14:48:08) [GCC 6.3.0 20170118]
```
##### CONFIGURATION

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
N/A

##### SUMMARY
`get_url`'s checksum parameter currently just takes a literal value. When using an artifact store such as artifactory, or the schema many open source projects follow however, `<url>.md5` or similar usually yields the checksum of an artifact. Other systems correctly set ETag to a unique identifier.

`get_url` should allow using this metadata to determine if a file has changed and to manage downloads accordingly. This would allow for efficient change detection behaviour without the associated network traffic.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
Proposal:
```yaml
# Full path to a sha1sum
- name: download foo.conf
  get_url:
    url: http://example.com/path/file.conf
    dest: /etc/foo.conf
    mode: 0440
    checksum_url: http://example.com/path/file.conf.sha1sum
# Checksum Header (send a HEAD request first)
- name: download foo.conf
  get_url:
    url: http://example.com/path/file.conf
    dest: /etc/foo.conf
    mode: 0440
    checksum_header: ETag
# Header caching (support when ETag is unique but nothing identifiable) - this would be an advanced use
- name: download foo.conf
  get_url:
    url: http://example.com/path/file.conf
    dest: /etc/foo.conf
    mode: 0440
    changed_header: ETag
    changed_cache_file: /root/file.conf.etag
# Check for changes using remote file, but confirm checksum is whatever we supply locally
# i.e. fail-fast - check local file, check if remote file checksum matches, download only if it would work.
# Makes large file failures fast.
- name: download foo.conf
  get_url:
    url: http://example.com/path/file.conf
    dest: /etc/foo.conf
    mode: 0440
    checksum_url: http://example.com/path/file.conf.sha1sum
    checksum: 4e1243bd22c66e76c2ba9eddc1f91394e57f9f83
```

title: apt-key module does not update expired keys
body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Feature Idea

##### COMPONENT NAME
<!--- Name of the module/plugin/task/feature -->
Module `apt_key`

##### ANSIBLE VERSION
<!--- Paste verbatim output from “ansible --version” between quotes below -->
```
ansible 2.2.1.0
  config file = ./ansible.cfg
  configured module search path = Default w/o overrides
```

##### CONFIGURATION
<!---
Mention any settings you have changed/added/removed in ansible.cfg
(or using the ANSIBLE_* environment variables).
-->
```
[defaults]
inventory           = local.ini
remote_user         = ansible
hash_behaviour      = merge

[ssh_connection]
ssh_args            = -o ControlMaster=auto -o ControlPersist=90s
control_path        = .ssh/%%h-%%p-%%r.master
```

##### OS / ENVIRONMENT
<!---
Mention the OS you are running Ansible from, and the OS you are
managing, or say “N/A” for anything that is not platform-specific.
-->
Ansible runs from Debian Stretch and is managing Debian Jessie nodes.

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->
I am reopening  #6693 which was closed due to repos migration.

I have encountered the same issue: a apt key gets expired and Ansible won't update it. Due to lack of the update feature apt wasn't possible to update cache and install/upgrade packages.

I need to workaround this feature by running `ansible` ad-hoc command removing this apt key and then running the *playbook* again.


##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
<!---
For bugs, show exactly how to reproduce the problem, using a minimal test-case.
For new features, show how the feature would be used.
-->

<!--- Paste example playbooks or commands between quotes below -->
```yaml
  - name: Fusion directory apt key
    apt_key:
      id: 62B4981F
      keyserver: 'keys.gnupg.net' 
```

##### EXPECTED RESULTS

When the key expires, download it again to check if new one is available.

##### ACTUAL RESULTS
<!--- What actually happened? If possible run with extra verbosity (-vvvv) -->

Nothing changed, Ansible reports `OK`.

<!--- Paste verbatim command output between quotes below -->
```
TASK [ldap : Fusion directory apt key] *************************************
ok: [node-jessie]
```

title: Upload binary file possibility in URI module (#3157)
body:
DETAILS SEE: https://github.com/ansible/ansible-modules-core/issues/3157




Issue Type:

    Feature Idea

Plugin Name:

uri
Ansible Version:

1.9.2
Environment:

N/A
Summary:

It would be nice to have option to upload binary file to HTTP endpoint using HTTP PUT. Expecting behaviour similar to following curl command:

curl -k -X PUT -u user:password --upload-file  test.jar "https://IP:PORT/endpoint/"

and example of possible URI usage:

- name: Push jar to nexus
  uri:
    url: "https://{{ inventory_hostname }}:{{ port }}/{{ endpoint }}"
    src: /tmp/{{ file }}.jar
    user: "{{ user }}"
    password: "{{ password }}"
    method: PUT
